target.title: Telegram: allow native channel commands in explicitly allowed chats
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: Telegram `channel_post` updates could carry regular text, but native slash commands such as `/status` were not authorized
correctly in channel relay setups.
- Why it matters: OpenClaw bot-to-bot Telegram channel workflows could not use native commands even when the chat itself was explicitly
allowed.
- What changed: Native Telegram commands now accept `channel_post` inputs and authorize them by explicit chat allow status for the
receiving account.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): This PR does not redefine sender-scoped `groups[chatId].allowFrom` semantics for `channel_post`,
because Telegram does not expose the originating human sender on that surface.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [x] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Integrations
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #49062

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Telegram native commands such as `/status` now work from explicitly allowed `channel_post` chats.
- Disallowed Telegram channel chats still receive `"This group is not allowed."`
- Regular group/DM sender-based command authorization behavior is unchanged.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`No`)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes`)
- Data access scope changed? (`No`)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

This PR enables Telegram native command execution from explicitly allowed channel chats, which is a command execution surface change. The
risk is bounded by existing explicit chat allow checks. Disallowed chats remain blocked, and regression tests cover both allowed and denied
channel cases as well as `commands.allowFrom.telegram` precedence.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

OpenClaw version: 2026.3.13
Operating system: all OS
Install method: npm global
Model: gpt-5.4
Provider / routing chain: chatgpt oauth



### Steps

1. Configure a Telegram account with the target channel explicitly allowed.
2. Send /status as a channel_post in that Telegram channel.
3. Observe native command authorization and reply behavior.

### Expected

- Explicitly allowed channel chats can execute native commands such as /status.

### Actual

- Before this fix, the command was rejected or dropped on channel_post.
- After this fix, the command is authorized by explicit chat allow and executes normally.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
    - /status from an explicitly allowed Telegram channel now succeeds.
    - Disallowed channel chats remain blocked by chat allowlist checks.
- Edge cases checked:
    - commands.allowFrom.telegram does not block explicitly allowed channel_post commands.
    - commands.allowFrom.telegram does not bypass denied channel_post chat allowlists.
- What you did not verify:
    - Sender-scoped groups[chatId].allowFrom semantics for channel_post
    - Live plugin command behavior on Telegram channels
    - Webhook-specific Telegram deployment paths

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for
maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (Yes)
- Config/env changes? (No)
- Migration needed? (No)
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly:
    - Revert this PR, or disable Telegram native commands temporarily with channels.telegram.commands.native: false
- Files/config to restore:
    - extensions/telegram/src/bot-native-commands.ts
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for:
    - Explicitly allowed Telegram channel /status still returns "You are not authorized to use this command."
    - Disallowed channel chats start executing native commands unexpectedly

## Risks and Mitigations

List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write None.

- Risk:
    - channel_post authorization now uses explicit chat allow rather than sender-scoped identity.
    - Mitigation:
        - This is limited to Telegram channel_post, where Telegram does not expose the originating human sender.
        - Explicit chat allow is still required.
        - Regression tests cover both allow and deny behavior.
- Risk:
    - Future contributors may assume sender-scoped groups[chatId].allowFrom also applies to channel_post.
    - Mitigation:
        - Scope is explicitly documented in this PR.
        - A separate follow-up issue will track channel_post sender-scoped allowlist semantics.

## Issue
解决 #28218: Feishu: fetch missed messages on reconnect

## 问题描述
当网关离线时（如电脑睡眠），飞书消息丢失。飞书 WebSocket 不像 Telegram 会重放离线消息。

## 解决方案
添加消息恢复服务，定期检查群聊消息，发现并恢复丢失的消息。

## 变更内容
新增文件：
- extensions/feishu/src/feishu-message-recovery.ts - 消息恢复模块

## 功能特性
- 定期检查消息（每30秒）
- 自动恢复丢失的消息
- 消息处理统计
- 支持手动触发恢复

target.title: test(gateway): consolidate deriveSessionTitle tests into dedicated module
target.body:
## Summary

This PR fixes an issue where heartbeat polls could overwrite explicit user-labeled session titles with transcript-derived titles like `"heartbeat"`.

## Changes

- Extracts `deriveSessionTitle` to `session-title.ts` with a dedicated test module.
- Preserves explicit session labels before transcript-derived fallbacks.
- Keeps auto-populated `origin.label` behind `firstUserMessage` so unlabeled inbound DMs can still use transcript-derived titles.
- Keeps `session-utils.ts` as a compatibility re-export site.
- Clarifies the zero timestamp test name to describe the actual `updatedAt: 0` input.

## Title precedence

1. `displayName`
2. `subject`
3. explicit `entry.label`
4. `firstUserMessage` (truncated)
5. auto-populated `entry.origin?.label`
6. `sessionId` prefix fallback

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: explicit session labels should survive heartbeat-style transcript summaries, while auto-populated origin labels should not override a real first user message.

Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on this branch after rebasing onto current `main`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
node --import tsx --eval 'import { deriveSessionTitle } from "./src/gateway/session-title.ts";
const cases = {
  explicitLabelBeatsHeartbeat: deriveSessionTitle({ sessionId: "abc123", updatedAt: Date.now(), label: "My App - Dashboard" }, "heartbeat"),
  transcriptBeatsAutoOriginLabel: deriveSessionTitle({ sessionId: "abc123", updatedAt: Date.now(), origin: { label: "Discord: Engineering" } }, "Need help with deployment"),
  originLabelFallbackWithoutTranscript: deriveSessionTitle({ sessionId: "abc123", updatedAt: Date.now(), origin: { label: "Discord: Engineering" } }, null),
  zeroUpdatedAtFallback: deriveSessionTitle({ sessionId: "abcd1234-5678-90ef-ghij-klmnopqrstuv", updatedAt: 0 }),
};
console.log(JSON.stringify(cases, null, 2));'
```

Evidence after fix:

```json
{
  "explicitLabelBeatsHeartbeat": "My App - Dashboard",
  "transcriptBeatsAutoOriginLabel": "Need help with deployment",
  "originLabelFallbackWithoutTranscript": "Discord: Engineering",
  "zeroUpdatedAtFallback": "abcd1234"
}
```

Observed result after fix: explicit labels win over heartbeat transcript text; transcript text wins over auto-populated origin labels; origin labels still act as fallback when there is no transcript; zero `updatedAt` keeps the session-id-only fallback.

What was not tested: full gateway UI rendering was not manually opened; this proof covers the extracted title derivation behavior directly.

## Validation

- `pnpm test src/gateway/session-title.test.ts src/gateway/session-utils.test.ts`
- `git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD`

target.title: ACP: add gateway-owned node-backed runtime
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: OpenClaw did not yet have a complete **node-backed ACP runtime** where the gateway owns the durable state and a paired node acts only as the execution worker.
- Why it matters: without that split, restart/replay/reconnect behavior is too dependent on the live process, which makes lease fencing, terminal resolution, and outward delivery recovery harder to reason about and prove.
- What this PR adds:
  - a durable ACP gateway store for sessions, runs, accepted worker events, leases, terminal state, replay targets, and projector checkpoints
  - gateway ingestion of ACP worker events coming back from paired nodes
  - a real node-host ACP worker bridge and gateway/node command plumbing
  - a durable ACP reply projection/replay path so restart recovery can resume from gateway state instead of only from the live iterator
  - startup recovery for incomplete ACP node runs
  - restart/reconnect/cancel/close race hardening with focused restart/replay test coverage
  - architecture / protocol / verification docs for the new backend
- Landing-only follow-up: after rebasing onto current `main`, this PR also includes a few **test-only** isolation fixes in Feishu / Google / Google Chat extension tests that were needed to make the landing branch green. Those are not ACP runtime behavior changes.

## What changed by area

### 1) Durable ACP gateway state

New ACP store/state plumbing under `src/acp/store/**`:

- persists ACP sessions and runs
- persists accepted worker events
- tracks leases / lease epochs
- resolves canonical terminal state on the gateway
- stores replay targets and projector checkpoints separately from the runtime cursor

This is the foundation that lets the gateway stay authoritative even when execution happens on a paired node.

### 2) Gateway ↔ node ACP worker path

Main implementation areas:

- `src/gateway/**`
- `src/infra/node-commands.ts`
- `src/node-host/**`

This adds the node-backed ACP execution path:

- gateway can start/control ACP work on a paired node
- node-host can run the ACP worker role
- worker events stream back to the gateway over the existing node transport
- gateway remains the source of truth for run state and terminal resolution

### 3) Durable reply projection and replay

Main implementation areas:

- `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.ts`
- `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-delivery.ts`
- `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-replay.ts`

This PR moves ACP outward delivery onto a durable replayable path:

- reply targets are persisted per run
- projector checkpoints are advanced only after successful delivery
- restart recovery can replay the unfinished suffix from durable state
- reconnect and duplicate accepted events do not re-deliver already completed outward effects

### 4) Startup recovery / reconnect handling

Main implementation areas:

- `src/gateway/server-startup.acp-node.ts`
- `src/gateway/server-node-events.ts`
- `src/acp/runtime/acp-node.ts`

This adds the gateway-side recovery flow needed to:

- resume incomplete projection after restart
- keep reconnect handling deterministic
- prevent stale-worker wins
- preserve canonical terminal behavior across restart/replay windows

### 5) Test harnesses and regression coverage

Main test additions/expansions:

- `src/acp/store/store.restart.test.ts`
- `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.replay.test.ts`
- `src/gateway/server-startup.acp-node.test.ts`
- `src/gateway/server-node-events.acp.test.ts`
- `src/node-host/invoke-acp.test.ts`
- `src/acp/test-harness/restart-harness.ts`

These cover the core failure windows this backend needs to survive:

- restart after accepted events but before replay checkpoint advancement
- restart after terminal persistence but before final outward convergence
- reconnect replay of already accepted events
- cancel / complete / close race handling
- restart recovery with a real memory cut instead of reusing in-process objects

### 6) Docs included in this PR

Main docs:

- `docs/experiments/plans/acp-node-backed-runtime.md`
- `docs/experiments/plans/acp-node-backed-runtime-protocol.md`
- `docs/experiments/plans/acp-node-backed-runtime-verification.md`
- `docs/experiments/plans/acp-node-runtime-program.md`
- `docs/experiments/plans/acp-node-slice4-summary.md`
- `docs/experiments/plans/acp-node-slice4-final-judge.md`

These are included because this was developed as a staged architecture / verification effort and the final accepted behavior is documented there.

## Explicit scope boundaries / v1 limitations

This PR does **not**:

- turn nodes into mini-gateways
- add automatic cross-node continuation for an in-flight run
- claim durable resumed tool-message edit support after restart

Current conservative post-restart tool-update behavior remains:

- `append_only_after_restart`

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [x] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related: ACP node runtime architecture / protocol / verification docs under `docs/experiments/plans/`
- Related: landing-only extension test isolation fixes after rebase onto current `main`

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- OpenClaw now has a first-class ACP node-backed runtime path where the gateway owns durable ACP truth and paired nodes execute the worker role.
- Gateway restart/recovery can resume incomplete ACP outward projection from durable state.
- Duplicate accepted worker-event replay on reconnect is hardened so it does not duplicate outward delivery.
- Post-restart tool updates intentionally follow the documented conservative v1 rule: `append_only_after_restart`.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? Yes
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? Yes
- Command/tool execution surface changed? Yes
- Data access scope changed? Yes

### Security / trust-boundary notes

- Paired nodes can now advertise ACP worker capability and receive ACP runtime commands over the existing gateway↔node trust boundary.
- Gateway↔node websocket traffic now carries ACP worker control / event flows in addition to existing node commands.
- Gateway now persists ACP runtime state needed for durable replay/recovery.
- The design mitigates stale-worker / duplicate-delivery risks via lease epochs, canonical terminal resolution, durable replay checkpoints, and reconnect/replay handling on the gateway.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux (landing worktree on vm1) + GitHub Actions CI
- Runtime/container: Node / pnpm; Bun build path also checked
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): ACP gateway↔node runtime path
- Relevant config (redacted): ACP runtime restart/replay harnesses and paired-node fixtures

### Verified locally

- ACP targeted verification gate:
  - `pnpm test -- src/auto-reply/reply/acp-projector.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-delivery.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.replay.test.ts src/acp/store/store.test.ts src/acp/store/store.restart.test.ts src/acp/store/gateway-events.test.ts src/gateway/server-node-events.acp.test.ts src/gateway/server-startup.acp-node.test.ts src/node-host/invoke-acp.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/nodes.acp.test.ts`
- TypeScript guardrail:
  - `pnpm tsgo`
- Landing-only extension test fixes:
  - `pnpm test:extensions`

### CI / build notes

- `pnpm build` still fails only with the pre-existing Bun wrapper issue:
  - `Cannot find module './cjs/index.cjs' from ''`
- That build failure was already present and is not introduced by the ACP runtime changes in this PR.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What I personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified the ACP-focused local gate listed above.
- Verified `pnpm tsgo` passes.
- Verified the rebased landing branch’s extension test-isolation fixes with local `pnpm test:extensions`.
- Verified the PR now includes the architecture / protocol / verification / final-judge docs for this backend.

What I did **not** verify:

- I did **not** get a clean `pnpm build` because of the pre-existing Bun wrapper failure noted above.
- I did **not** run a fresh manual end-to-end Discord/real-node interactive repro outside the checked-in restart/replay and node-host harnesses.

## Review Guide

If you want the shortest path through this PR, review in this order:

1. **Core architecture / intent**
   - `docs/experiments/plans/acp-node-backed-runtime.md`
   - `docs/experiments/plans/acp-node-backed-runtime-protocol.md`
2. **Gateway-owned durable state**
   - `src/acp/store/**`
3. **Node execution bridge**
   - `src/node-host/**`
   - `src/gateway/server-methods/nodes.ts`
   - `src/gateway/server-node-events.ts`
4. **Durable outward replay path**
   - `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.ts`
   - `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-delivery.ts`
   - `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-replay.ts`
5. **Recovery and proofs**
   - `src/gateway/server-startup.acp-node.ts`
   - `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.replay.test.ts`
   - `src/gateway/server-startup.acp-node.test.ts`
6. **Ignore as landing-only test fixes unless you are checking CI fallout**
   - `extensions/feishu/**`
   - `extensions/google/**`
   - `extensions/googlechat/**`

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No explicit migration required
- Migration needed? No

Notes:

- This adds a new ACP runtime/backend path; it does not force existing deployments to switch to it immediately.
- Post-restart tool updates intentionally remain append-only in v1.

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert this PR or avoid selecting/using the `acp-node` runtime path until fixed.
- Files/config to restore: ACP runtime/store/gateway/node-host changes under `src/acp/**`, `src/node-host/**`, `src/gateway/**`, plus the accompanying docs if reverting wholesale.
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: duplicate final delivery after restart, stale worker winning after reconnect, projector checkpoint drift, or replay targeting the wrong route.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: restart/replay correctness is subtle in any durable projection system.
  - Mitigation: this PR includes restart harnesses, startup recovery tests, reconnect/replay ordering tests, and explicit protocol/verification docs.
- Risk: the PR is large and spans multiple ACP runtime layers.
  - Mitigation: the review guide above splits it into durable store, node bridge, replay path, recovery path, and landing-only CI fixes.
- Risk: paired-node ACP execution expands the gateway↔node command surface.
  - Mitigation: the gateway remains authoritative for leases, terminal resolution, checkpoints, and replay instead of trusting node-local completion.

---

## AI Checklist

- [x] **AI-assisted** — agent-harness / Codex driven implementation, review, and landing follow-ups
- [x] **Testing degree** — focused local verification plus CI
- [x] **Understanding confirmed** — this PR adds the gateway-owned ACP node-backed runtime and includes a few landing-only extension test fixes after rebase

target.title: Cron: constrain main-session system events
target.body:
# PR Body — cron-wrapper remediation

## Summary
This PR ships a narrow safety remediation for the Telegram/main-session contamination class caused by cron/systemEvent reminder handling.

Specifically, it fixes two linked bottlenecks:
1. dangerous relay-style wrapper prose entering the main-session prompt path for cron reminders
2. rich `sessionTarget:"main"` `systemEvent` reminder/follow-up prose being accepted on the main-session path

The patch is intentionally narrow and keeps isolated follow-up flows intact.

---

## Problem
A prior incident showed that rich main-session cron/systemEvent reminder text could be wrapped into natural-language relay-style prompt content and then persist as transcript residue in a long-lived main Telegram session.

That contamination, combined with compaction timeout/fallback behavior, contributed to repeated stale-response behavior.

This PR addresses the transcript-contaminating reminder path directly, without reopening the broader compaction-guard architecture.

---

## What changed
### 1) Neutralize dangerous main-session reminder wrapper text
The heartbeat/cron event prompt path no longer emits the old relay-style reminder prose.
Instead, main-session cron wakes now use a much more minimal internal wake shape.

### 2) Constrain rich main-session `systemEvent` text
Main-session cron `systemEvent` text is now constrained/rejected when it looks like reminder/relay/follow-up prose.
Unsafe legacy-ish main-session text degrades to a safe wake token path rather than replaying rich prose into the main transcript.

### 3) Preserve safe isolated flows
Rich follow-up behavior is still supported through isolated `agentTurn` jobs, which remain the preferred path.

---

## Key touched surfaces
- `src/infra/heartbeat-events-filter.ts`
- `src/cron/main-session-system-event.ts`
- `src/cron/normalize.ts`
- `src/cron/service/jobs.ts`
- `src/agents/tools/cron-tool.ts`
- focused tests around cron/heartbeat/main-session behavior

---

## Validation
Targeted Vitest suites pass:
- **153 / 153 tests passed**
- 6 focused test files

Final review outcome on the restacked branch: **ready**

---

## Compatibility / behavior notes
- rich main-session `systemEvent` reminder prose is intentionally no longer supported
- unsafe legacy-ish main-session text degrades to a safe wake token path
- rich follow-ups should use isolated `agentTurn` jobs instead of main-session prose wakes

---

## Why this patch is narrow
This branch is restacked onto `main` as a single narrow remediation unit focused on cron/heartbeat/main-session safety, rather than bundling the earlier compaction-guard stack.

target.title: fix(tui): handle German AltGr input
target.body:
## Summary
- ignore Kitty printable key-release events in the TUI custom editor so release events cannot duplicate text
- decode German-layout AltGr Kitty CSI-u printable input when terminals report Ctrl+Alt plus a base-layout key
- add regression coverage for AltGr @/€, ordinary Alt-modified input, and printable release events

## Verification
- pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/tui/components/custom-editor.ts src/tui/components/custom-editor.test.ts src/tui/tui.ts src/tui/tui.test.ts CHANGELOG.md
- node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/tui/components/custom-editor.test.ts src/tui/tui.test.ts
- pnpm check:changed
- git diff --check

## Not Run
- Live German keyboard / Kitty-terminal smoke test; this environment does not have that keyboard layout attached.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: TUI input should accept German-layout AltGr Kitty CSI-u printable characters such as `@` and `€`, while ignoring printable key-release events so releases do not duplicate text. This covers issue #48897 in `src/tui/components/custom-editor.ts`.
- Real environment tested: Local Linux 6.19.14+kali-amd64 isolated worktree at PR head `6dfa6f45a0`; Node v22.22.2; pnpm 11.1.0. The smoke used a pseudo-terminal, raw stdin bytes, and the real `CustomEditor.handleInput(...)` path with `@earendil-works/pi-tui` key decoding.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  1. `git switch --detach origin/main` in the isolated proof worktree, then ran a Python PTY script that spawned `node --import tsx --input-type=module`, constructed `CustomEditor`, set stdin raw, and wrote Kitty CSI-u bytes to the PTY.
  2. Repeated the same PTY/raw-stdin script at PR head `origin/pr-83947`.
  3. `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.tui.config.ts src/tui/components/custom-editor.test.ts`
- Evidence after fix: Terminal output from the PTY smoke:
  ```
  current main input bytes: ^[[64::113;7u ^[[8364::101;7u ^[[214;1u ^[[214;1:3u
  current main RESULT {"text":"ÖÖ","length":2}
  PR head input bytes:    ^[[64::113;7u ^[[8364::101;7u ^[[214;1u ^[[214;1:3u
  PR head RESULT {"text":"@€Ö","length":3}

  node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.tui.config.ts src/tui/components/custom-editor.test.ts
  ✓ tui src/tui/components/custom-editor.test.ts (5 tests) 89ms
  Test Files 1 passed (1)
  Tests 5 passed (5)
  ```
- Observed result after fix: The same raw Kitty CSI-u stream that produced `ÖÖ` on current main produced `@€Ö` on the PR branch: AltGr `@` and `€` were inserted, the ordinary `Ö` press was inserted once, and the `Ö` release event was ignored instead of duplicating the character.
- What was not tested: Did not test a physical German keyboard or the live Kitty terminal application; this environment does not have that keyboard layout attached. The smoke exercised the exact CSI-u bytes through a PTY/raw-stdin path instead.

Fixes openclaw/openclaw#48897

target.title: feat(whatsapp): add poll, reaction, event, and RSVP message support
target.body:
## Summary

- Add `pollCreationMessage` (V1/V2/V3) recognition → renders as `<media:poll>` with question and numbered options
- Add `pollUpdateMessage` recognition → renders as `<media:poll-vote>` placeholder
- Add `reactionMessage` recognition → renders as `<reaction:emoji>` placeholder
- Add `eventMessage` recognition → renders as `<media:event>` with name, description, and cancellation status
- Add `eventResponseMessage` (RSVP) recognition → renders as `<event-rsvp:going|not-going|maybe>` with guest count
- Add `encEventResponseMessage` (encrypted RSVP) recognition → renders as `<event-rsvp:encrypted>`
- Add `secretEncryptedMessage` (event closing/cancellation) → renders as `<media:encrypted-update>`
- Subscribe to Baileys `messages.update` event for poll vote updates with correct voter identity
- Subscribe to Baileys `messages.reaction` event for emoji reactions with correct reactor identity
- Route all new message types through the full inbound pipeline (normalize → enrich → enqueue)
- Properly clean up all event listeners in `close()` to prevent memory leaks on reconnect
- **Fire `message:received` internal hooks for gated group messages** so hook handlers observe all messages even when `requireMention: true` skips auto-reply

## Problem

1. WhatsApp poll creation messages, poll votes, emoji reactions, event creation/RSVP messages, and encrypted event updates are silently dropped by the inbound message pipeline.
2. When `requireMention: true` is set for groups, the `message:received` internal hook never fires for unmentioned messages, making hook-based observability (logging, analytics, poll tracking) impossible.

## Root Cause

**Message type support:** In `extract.ts`, `extractText()` and `extractMediaPlaceholder()` don't recognize poll/reaction/event message types, causing `enrichInboundMessage()` to return `null`.

**Hook gating:** In `on-message.ts`, `applyGroupGating()` returns `{shouldProcess: false}` for unmentioned messages and the handler returns early, before the message reaches `dispatch-from-config.ts` where `message:received` hooks fire.

## Changes

### `extensions/whatsapp/src/inbound/extract.ts`
- Add poll creation (V1/V2/V3), poll vote, reaction, event, RSVP, encrypted RSVP, and secret encrypted message placeholders

### `extensions/whatsapp/src/inbound/monitor.ts`
- Subscribe to `messages.update` with correct voter identity from `pollUpdateMessageKey`
- Subscribe to `messages.reaction` with correct reactor identity
- Clean up listeners in `close()`

### `extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply/monitor/on-message.ts`
- Fire `message:received` internal hook via `fireAndForgetHook(triggerInternalHook(...))` in the `!gating.shouldProcess` early-return path
- Uses the same pattern as `dispatch-from-config.ts` for consistency
- Fire-and-forget: errors in hook handlers cannot affect the gating flow

### `extensions/whatsapp/src/inbound.test.ts`
- Comprehensive tests for all new message types

## Test plan

- [x] All 281 WhatsApp extension tests pass (36 files)
- [x] Format check (oxfmt) passes
- [x] Type check passes
- [x] Poll creation → `<media:poll>` with question and options
- [x] Poll vote → `<media:poll-vote>` with voter identity
- [x] Reaction → `<reaction:emoji>` with reactor identity
- [x] Event → `<media:event>` with name and description
- [x] RSVP → `<event-rsvp:going|not-going|maybe>`
- [x] Hooks fire for gated group messages (requireMention: true)
- [x] No auto-reply sent for unmentioned group messages
- [x] No hook duplication for mentioned/non-gated messages

Closes #12197
Closes #7080
Relates to #21905

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: Telegram: add reaction notification wake policy
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Telegram reaction events were queued as passive context, so reaction-only inputs did not wake the routed agent session.
- Why it matters: operators may want selected notification events to wake a routed session, but making every accepted reaction wake by default is a compatibility change.
- What changed: accepted Telegram reaction events now include stable `reaction_key` values for standard and custom emoji and route through shared `notificationWake.reactions` policy.
- Compatibility boundary: reaction events stay queue-only by default; set `channels.telegram.notificationWake.reactions: "wake"` to request immediate routed heartbeat wakes.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #47677
- Related: #83611
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: accepted Telegram reaction-only events now enqueue stable reaction-key system events and wake the routed session only when the resolved shared notification wake policy is `wake`. Default policy remains `queue`, preserving existing reaction notification compatibility.
- Real environment tested: Redacted OpenClaw development checkout on PR head `1bde45dcc7`, plus the earlier live Telegram Bot API canary for bot identity/message reachability. No secrets, usernames, chat titles, local paths, or raw IDs are included here.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/notification-system-events.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.test.ts`
  - `pnpm config:channels:check`
  - `pnpm config:docs:check`
  - `pnpm tsgo:prod`
  - `pnpm test:contracts:plugins`
  - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 <touched files>`
  - `git diff --check`
- Evidence after fix: Redacted terminal verification from PR head:

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/notification-system-events.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.test.ts
Test Files  3 passed (3)
Tests  159 passed (159)

pnpm config:channels:check
passed

pnpm config:docs:check
OK docs/.generated/config-baseline.sha256

pnpm tsgo:prod
passed

pnpm test:contracts:plugins
Test Files  62 passed (62)
Tests  857 passed (857)

pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 <touched files>
All matched files use the correct format.

git diff --check
passed
```

Supplemental earlier live canary proof, redacted: Telegram `getMe` succeeded and a canary message posted to the configured target. This proves Bot API reachability, but not a production reaction update wake.
- Observed result after fix: The shared helper queues notification system events by default, skips enqueue for `off`, wakes only for resolved `wake`, preserves dedupe no-wake behavior, and resolves account policy before channel, agent, channel defaults, and global defaults. Telegram handler tests prove queue-by-default, opt-in wake, custom emoji stable keys, trust downgrade, authorization/routing preservation, and no wake when enqueue deduplicates.
- What was not tested: I did not capture a live Telegram Desktop reaction update waking a production routed session after this policy patch. The PR still has the `mantis: telegram-visible-proof` label so maintainer/Mantis proof can cover that external surface.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): current main enqueues accepted Telegram reaction system events and returns without requesting a heartbeat wake.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Telegram `message_reaction` handling enqueued system events and then returned without a policy-controlled wake path.
- Missing detection / guardrail: existing tests asserted enqueue behavior but did not assert notification wake policy, default queue compatibility, or deduped no-wake behavior.
- Contributing context (if known): Telegram custom emoji reactions were not represented in the event text because only standard emoji reactions were collected.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Contract test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target tests/files: `src/infra/notification-system-events.test.ts`, `extensions/telegram/src/bot.test.ts`, `extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.test.ts`, `pnpm test:contracts:plugins`
- Scenario the test should lock in: accepted standard/custom emoji reactions enqueue stable reaction-key events, stay queue-only by default, wake only under `notificationWake.reactions: "wake"`, and do not wake when enqueue deduplicates.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the shared helper owns policy precedence and heartbeat behavior; the Telegram handler harness owns reaction authorization/routing/enqueue behavior.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Telegram reaction notifications that pass existing access controls enqueue system events by default. Operators can opt into immediate routed wakes with `channels.telegram.notificationWake.reactions: "wake"`. System event text includes `reaction_key=emoji:<emoji>` or `reaction_key=custom_emoji:<id>` so preference/prompt text can assign meaning to specific reactions without adding per-emoji channel schema.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Default:
[Telegram reaction] -> [system event queued] -> [processed on next scheduled/explicit wake]

Opt-in wake:
[Telegram reaction] -> [notificationWake.reactions = wake] -> [system event queued] -> [heartbeat wake] -> [agent sees reaction event]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: redacted local development environment
- Runtime/container: local source checkout
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Telegram handler unit harness and shared notification system-event helper
- Relevant config (redacted): `channels.telegram.reactionNotifications: "all"`, `dmPolicy: "open"`, `allowFrom: ["*"]`, and opt-in wake case with `notificationWake.reactions: "wake"`

### Steps

1. Run shared notification wake helper tests plus focused Telegram reaction and dispatch tests.
2. Run generated config metadata and docs checks.
3. Run production typecheck and plugin contract tests.
4. Run format and whitespace checks.

### Expected

- Default reaction notifications enqueue without wake.
- `notificationWake.reactions: "wake"` wakes the routed session after enqueue.
- `notificationWake.reactions: "off"` skips notification enqueue.
- Deduped notification events do not wake.
- Typecheck, generated config checks, plugin contracts, format, and whitespace checks pass.

### Actual

- Passed locally on PR head `1bde45dcc7`.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: queue-by-default reaction notification, explicit wake policy, off policy, account policy precedence, dedupe no-wake, standard emoji stable key, custom emoji stable key, existing authorization/routing cases.
- Edge cases checked: reaction removals ignored, `own`/`all`/`off` reaction modes, bot-user reactions ignored, forum/general topic routing remains unchanged.
- What you did **not** verify: live Telegram Desktop user reaction update waking a production routed session.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? Yes, optional `notificationWake.reactions` policy surface
- Migration needed? No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: reaction notifications could start an agent turn unexpectedly.
  - Mitigation: default policy is `queue`; immediate wake requires explicit `notificationWake.reactions: "wake"` and remains gated by existing Telegram sender/access controls.
- Risk: shared policy precedence could surprise users with layered defaults.
  - Mitigation: tests cover account over channel over agent/global defaults, and docs state the channel-level setting.

target.title: perf(infra): cache sync JSON reads with stat validation
target.body:
## Summary

Sync JSON reads dominate TUI cold start — profiled startup made ~210K `tryReadJsonSync` calls against ~380 unique paths, with each bundled plugin's `package.json` re-read ~1500 times. This adds a process-scoped, stat-validated memo as an **opt-in** fast path and leaves the legacy uncached API alone.

- **New cached entrypoints** in `src/infra/json-files.ts`: `tryReadJsonSync`, `readRootJsonObjectSync`. Each call does one `statSync` and returns the cached parse when `(mtime, size, inode)` matches. Atomic writes, external edits, and deletions evict naturally.
- **Legacy `loadJsonFile` (`src/infra/json-file.ts`) bypasses the cache.** All its callers — subagent registry store (own stat-signature cache), `cli-credentials`, `auth-profiles/*`, `current-conversation-bindings`, `doctor-auth-*` — either run their own invalidation or are auth/transient state where a stat-validated cache hit across re-auth would be wrong.
- TUI startup hot paths can opt in by importing directly from `json-files.ts`.

Disable globally with `OPENCLAW_DISABLE_JSON_READ_CACHE=1` or call `clearJsonReadCache()`.

## Behavior addressed

TUI cold start spent 20–30s in 200K+ sync JSON reads hammering the same plugin manifests. After this, each unique file is read+parsed once; subsequent calls cost a single `statSync` (~5–10µs) instead of open + read + parse (~50–100µs).

## Real environment tested

macOS, Node 24, `pnpm openclaw` cold launch.

## Exact steps or command run after this patch

```
pnpm build
pnpm openclaw
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/session-utils.subagent.test.ts src/infra/json-file.test.ts src/infra/json-files.test.ts
```

## Evidence after fix

- TUI responsive in single-digit seconds vs. 20–30s freeze on `main`.
- Vitest green: 81 tests in `session-utils.subagent.test.ts` (including the "reuses one subagent registry disk snapshot" invariant) + 23 tests across `json-file(s)`.

## What was not tested

- Long-running session soak around atomic writes / external edits.
- Crabbox / Testbox broader suites (CI on PR).

## Trade-off

`src/plugins/CLAUDE.md` cautions against fs-primitive metadata caches. By keeping the cache opt-in and leaving `loadJsonFile` callers uncached, the surface area is narrow: auth/credential and registry paths are unaffected, and the cache only fires where TUI startup actually benefits. The structural fix is to memoize at the snapshot/registry layer — that work continues in #84283 / #84302 / #84339. This PR is the pragmatic floor.

target.title: fix(discord): preserve streamed replies after tool warnings
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Discord draft-preview finalization could clear a streamed final answer when a later failed-tool warning arrived as an error final.
- Solution: keep later error finals out of the draft-preview finalizer after a non-error final has started delivery, while still delivering the warning normally.
- What changed: added a bounded Discord delivery state guard and a regression test for final answer plus later tool-warning delivery.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): standalone error finals still use the existing draft cleanup path; no Discord config or streaming defaults changed.

## Motivation

- Fixes a user-visible Discord streaming failure where the final answer can be replaced by or cleared after a later tool-call failure warning.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #83831
- Related #83844
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

Behavior addressed: Discord streamed final replies should remain visible when a later failed-tool warning is delivered.
Real environment tested: Source checkout on macOS with focused Discord monitor tests; pre-patch AWS Crabbox attempts from current main did not reproduce the exact destructive deletion state.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.process.test.ts; pnpm check:changed
Evidence after fix: The new regression test keeps the preview message id after delivering a normal final followed by an isError final, and the full Discord process test file passes.
Observed result after fix: The finalized preview remains intact and the failed-tool warning is delivered through normal Discord reply delivery.
What was not tested: A successful after-patch live AWS Discord deletion reproduction/fix comparison, because current main did not reliably reproduce the destructive live state during investigation.
Before evidence (optional but encouraged): The new regression test failed before the fix because the later error final cleared the already-finalized draft preview.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Discord final delivery let later error finals re-enter the finalizable draft-preview adapter after a non-error final had already promoted the draft preview into the visible answer.
- Missing detection / guardrail: There was no regression test for multiple final payloads where a normal answer is followed by a failed-tool warning.
- Contributing context (if known): Shared finalizer fallback cleanup is correct for standalone error finals, but not after a successful preview finalization has already made the draft durable.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.process.test.ts
- Scenario the test should lock in: Discord partial streaming finalizes a preview, then a later isError final is delivered without clearing the preview.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The failure is in Discord monitor delivery/finalizer coordination and can be reproduced with the existing process test harness.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Discord streamed replies should no longer disappear when a later failed-tool warning is delivered.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
final answer -> preview finalized -> later tool warning -> preview cleanup clears answer

After:
final answer -> preview finalized -> later tool warning delivered normally -> answer remains visible
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No
- If any Yes, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS local checkout; AWS Crabbox was used during pre-patch investigation
- Runtime/container: Node/Vitest local test harness
- Model/provider: N/A for regression test
- Integration/channel (if any): Discord
- Relevant config (redacted): Discord streamMode partial in the regression test

### Steps

1. Run the focused Discord process test file.
2. Verify the new regression test finalizes a preview, sends a later isError final, and does not clear the preview.
3. Run changed typecheck/lint/guards.

### Expected

- The final answer preview remains visible and the warning is delivered separately.

### Actual

- The final answer preview remains visible and the warning is delivered separately.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: new regression failed before the fix and passed after; full Discord process test file passed; changed checks passed.
- Edge cases checked: standalone error final behavior remains covered by the adjacent existing test.
- What you did not verify: post-patch live AWS Discord reproduction, because the exact destructive live state was not reliably reproducible on current main.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: accidentally changing standalone error-final draft cleanup.
  - Mitigation: the guard only skips draft-preview finalization for an error final that follows a non-error final delivery start; existing standalone error-final coverage remains intact.

target.title: fix(anthropic): preserve configured Claude image capability
target.body:
## Summary
- route configured/default `models list` rows through provider-owned resolved-model normalization before displaying model capabilities
- keep Anthropic Claude 4 image capability policy in `extensions/anthropic` instead of duplicating Claude prefix data in core
- cover the `anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-5` configured/default path while preserving generic configured-row fallback behavior
- add changelog entry

## Real behavior proof
- **Behavior addressed:** A configured default model such as `agents.defaults.model = "anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-5"` could still resolve through a stale text-only configured/catalog path, causing image input to be treated as unsupported even after the provider-discovered row fix.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS, PR head `3bc1284add`, using an isolated `OPENCLAW_HOME=/private/tmp/openclaw-83756-proof` and `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/private/tmp/openclaw-83756-proof/.openclaw/openclaw.json` with only `agents.defaults.model` set to `anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-5`.
- **Exact steps or command run after the patch:**
  ```sh
  env OPENCLAW_HOME=/private/tmp/openclaw-83756-proof \
    OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/private/tmp/openclaw-83756-proof/.openclaw/openclaw.json \
    PATH=/Users/andy/.cache/codex-runtimes/codex-primary-runtime/dependencies/node/bin:/opt/homebrew/bin:/usr/bin:/bin:/usr/sbin:/sbin \
    pnpm openclaw models list --json
  ```
- **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from the real OpenClaw CLI after the patch:
  ```json
  {
    "count": 1,
    "models": [
      {
        "key": "anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-5",
        "name": "claude-sonnet-4-5",
        "input": "text+image",
        "contextWindow": 200000,
        "local": false,
        "available": false,
        "tags": [
          "default"
        ],
        "missing": false
      }
    ]
  }
  ```
- **Observed result after fix:** The configured default Anthropic Claude 4 row now reports `input: "text+image"` instead of text-only. The configured/default list row reaches the existing Anthropic provider-owned `normalizeResolvedModel` hook, so the same plugin-owned capability policy is used for this stale catalog path.
- **What was not tested:** I did not make a live Anthropic API request. This proof covers the local configured model capability resolution and CLI-visible configured/default path that the reporter identified; the live provider call still depends on valid Anthropic credentials.

## Validation
- `pnpm test src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/model.inline-provider.test.ts src/commands/models.list.e2e.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/model.inline-provider.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/model.inline-provider.test.ts src/commands/models/list.rows.ts src/commands/models/list.list-command.ts src/commands/models.list.e2e.test.ts CHANGELOG.md`
- `git diff --check`

Follow-up for https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/83756#issuecomment-4487709808.

target.title: fix(cron): emit message.queued/processed for isolated-agent turns
target.body:
## Summary

The diagnostic-event seam for `message.queued` / `session.state` / `message.processed` is currently emitted only by channel-driven dispatch (`src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.ts:417`) and the embedded runner (`src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/runs.ts:75,89`). The isolated-cron agent path (`src/cron/isolated-agent/run.ts`) executes full agent turns including LLM calls, but never emits these events. Anything subscribed to the diagnostic-events bus for per-session attribution silently drops 100% of cron-isolated traffic.

This PR adds a paired `logMessageQueued` → `logSessionStateChange("processing")` → ... → `logSessionStateChange("idle")` → `logMessageProcessed` lifecycle inside `runCronIsolatedAgentTurn`, using the prepared `runSessionId` / `runSessionKey` and a stable `cron-isolated` source label. This mirrors the dispatch path's lifecycle so `state.queueDepth` is incremented and decremented symmetrically and downstream subscribers see the full per-run lifecycle.

## Problem

Any consumer of `message.queued` for per-session attribution — diagnostics exporters, OTel bridges, custom observability plugins — currently sees zero spans for cron-isolated runs. LLM calls fire, sessions exist, but downstream subscribers cannot distinguish runs from each other and tend to bucket them under whatever static fallback id their integration uses.

In a deployment with several daily isolated cron jobs over a 13-day window, an OTel subscriber observed 63 distinct cron-driven sessions in the native JSONL shipper but only 4 distinct `session.id` values reaching its trace store — a 94% gap that this single emission point closes.

## Solution

Added paired diagnostic-event calls to `runCronIsolatedAgentTurn`:

- `logMessageQueued({sessionId, sessionKey, channel: "cron", source: "cron-isolated"})` after `prepared.ok`.
- `logSessionStateChange({state: "processing"})` immediately after queued.
- `logSessionStateChange({state: "idle"})` in `finally` (decrements `state.queueDepth`).
- `logMessageProcessed({outcome, durationMs, error?})` in `finally`.

Outcomes: success → `completed`; aborted/thrown/finalizer-error → `error` with reason.

## Changes

- `src/cron/isolated-agent/run.ts` — added the diagnostic lifecycle calls inside `runCronIsolatedAgentTurn`.
- `src/cron/isolated-agent/run.diagnostic-events.test.ts` — new focused regression test asserting the queued → processing → idle → processed sequence with consistent `sessionKey`.
- `CHANGELOG.md` — entry under `## Unreleased` / `### Fixes`.

## Real Behavior Proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Subscribers to OpenClaw's diagnostic-events bus (e.g. OTel exporters) receive zero `message.queued` / `message.processed` events for isolated-cron agent turns, even though those runs make LLM calls and have a real session UUID. Downstream attribution buckets every cron-isolated run under whatever static fallback id the integration declares, hiding distinct sessions from traces.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout built from this branch (`pr/cron-isolated-message-queued`, head `275f1bb430`) on Linux 6.12.30 / Node 24.13.0. Gateway run as a systemd user unit (`openclaw-gateway.service`). A diagnostic-events subscriber registered via `onDiagnosticEvent` was attached to verify event emission per run.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
pnpm install
pnpm tsgo:core
pnpm test src/cron/isolated-agent/run.diagnostic-events.test.ts
pnpm test src/cron/isolated-agent/run.session-key.test.ts \
          src/cron/isolated-agent/run.session-key-isolation.test.ts \
          src/cron/isolated-agent/run.payload-fallbacks.test.ts \
          src/cron/isolated-agent/run.meta-error-status.test.ts
pnpm build
systemctl --user restart openclaw-gateway
node dist/index.js cron run <isolated-job-id>
journalctl --user -u openclaw-gateway --since "30 seconds ago" \
  | grep -E "event: message\\.(queued|processed)|started root span"
```

- **Evidence after fix:** Subscriber output from the live deployment after triggering an isolated cron job (session UUIDs and job ids redacted to short prefixes; agent label is generic, no IPs / chat ids / private endpoints):

```
[bridge] event: message.queued sessionKey: agent:main:cron:<jobId-prefix>:run:<runUuid-prefix> source: cron-isolated
[bridge] started root span for main session: agent:main:cron:<jobId-prefix>:run:<runUuid-prefix> agent: <agentId>
[bridge] event: message.processed sessionKey: agent:main:cron:<jobId-prefix>:run:<runUuid-prefix>
```

Tests:

```
pnpm tsgo:core              → exit 0
pnpm test src/cron/isolated-agent/run.diagnostic-events.test.ts
  Test Files  1 passed (1)
       Tests  1 passed (1)

pnpm test src/cron/isolated-agent/run.session-key.test.ts \
          src/cron/isolated-agent/run.session-key-isolation.test.ts \
          src/cron/isolated-agent/run.payload-fallbacks.test.ts \
          src/cron/isolated-agent/run.meta-error-status.test.ts
  Test Files  4 passed (4)
       Tests  17 passed (17)
```

- **Observed result after fix:** Both lifecycle events fire with the run's `sessionId` / `sessionKey`. The bridge subscriber attached via `onDiagnosticEvent` receives `message.queued`, then `session.state` (processing → idle), then `message.processed`, all sharing the same `sessionKey` for the same isolated cron run. `state.queueDepth` returns to 0 after the run because the paired `logSessionStateChange("idle")` runs in `finally`. Distinct cron run UUIDs now reach downstream span/aggregation logic instead of collapsing into a single static fallback id.
- **What was not tested:** The heartbeat-runner isolated-session path at `src/infra/heartbeat-runner.ts:1266` shares a similar shape but is intentionally out of scope for this PR — left as a follow-up if maintainers agree the same seam belongs there. `pnpm check` was not run end-to-end; the changed-surface gates above plus the build are the proof bar this PR carries.

## Notes

- `logMessageQueued` runs after `prepared.ok` to avoid noisy queued/processed pairs for runs that bail out at preparation; matches the existing pattern where dispatch only logs after route resolution.
- Both `logSessionStateChange` calls and `logMessageProcessed` run inside the `finally` block, ensuring the `state.queueDepth` decrement and processed event always fire even on early returns / throws.
- No public-API changes; `logMessageQueued` / `logSessionStateChange` / `logMessageProcessed` are existing exports.

## Review feedback addressed (rev 2, commit 275f1bb430)

- **P1 — taskLabel:** dropped. The `logMessageQueued` call now passes only `{sessionId, sessionKey, channel, source}`, matching the helper's typed parameter and event schema on `main`.
- **P2 — queue-state lifecycle:** added `logSessionStateChange({state: "processing"})` immediately after `logMessageQueued`, and `logSessionStateChange({state: "idle"})` in the `finally` block before `logMessageProcessed`. This pairs the `state.queueDepth` increment with its decrement and matches the dispatch path's lifecycle.
- **P3 — changelog:** added entry under `## Unreleased` / `### Fixes`.
- **Real behavior proof:** subscriber output included above (redacted).
- **Regression test:** added `run.diagnostic-events.test.ts` covering queued / processing / idle / processed sequencing and session-key consistency.

target.title: fix(backup): dereference archive hardlinks
target.body:
## Summary
- disable tar hardlink emission during backup creation so hardlinked files are archived as regular entries
- include hardlink targets in backup verification and reject traversal/absolute targets
- add regression coverage for hardlinked backup payloads and unsafe Link headers

## Verification
- pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/infra/backup-create.ts src/infra/backup-create.test.ts src/commands/backup-verify.ts src/commands/backup-verify.test.ts src/commands/backup.ts src/commands/backup.test.ts src/commands/backup.create-verify.test.ts src/commands/backup.atomic.test.ts CHANGELOG.md
- node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/backup-create.test.ts src/commands/backup-verify.test.ts src/commands/backup.test.ts src/commands/backup.create-verify.test.ts src/commands/backup.atomic.test.ts
- pnpm check:changed
- git diff --check

## Not Run
- Live macOS bsdtar restore smoke; this environment is Linux.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Backup archives should dereference source hardlinked files so a backup containing two hardlinked source paths stores two regular file entries under the archive root, not a tar hardlink entry whose link target can be outside or invalid after archive path remapping.
- Real environment tested: Linux 6.19.14+kali-amd64, Node v22.22.2, pnpm 10.33.2; isolated worktree at PR head `53802c6a93f6`; real filesystem hardlink created with `fs.link(...)` inside an isolated `OPENCLAW_HOME`/`OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`.

  3. Cross-checked the generated archive with system tar: `tar -tvf /tmp/openclaw-backup-hardlink-proof-zGSJ13/out/hardlink-proof.tar.gz`.
- Evidence after fix: Terminal output from the smoke command:

- Observed result after fix: The smoke command exited 0. The source files were real hardlinks with the same inode and `nlink=2`. The archive contained no tar hardlink entries, both source names were archived as regular `File` entries with no linkpath, OpenClaw backup verification passed, and extraction produced equal file contents but separate inodes with `nlink=1`.

target.title: docs(imessage): warn that cliPath wrappers must stream JSON-RPC stdio (#84330)
target.body:
Fixes #84330.

`docs/channels/imessage.md` documents that `channels.imessage.cliPath` can be pointed at an SSH/custom wrapper but doesn't call out a critical interoperability requirement: the wrapper must forward JSON-RPC stdin/stdout incrementally. The OpenClaw iMessage channel exchanges small newline-framed JSON-RPC frames over the wrapper's stdio for the channel's entire lifetime. A wrapper that reads with a large blocking `read(N)` or buffers stdin until EOF will make small frames like `chats.list` sit indefinitely, producing symptoms that look exactly like an iMessage outage (`imsg rpc timeout (chats.list)`, repeated channel restarts) even though `imsg rpc` itself is fine.

## Changes

- `docs/channels/imessage.md`: add a `<Warning>` block inside the "Remote Mac over SSH" Tab listing the six interoperability requirements the issue calls out (forward stdin/stdout promptly, preserve newlines, don't wait for EOF, avoid fixed-size blocking reads, keep stderr separate). Cite the exact symptom phrasing the issue uses (`imsg rpc timeout`) so operators searching for that error find the warning. Note the practical pitfall that pipelines like `ssh host imsg | grep ...` need `stdbuf -oL -eL` on every stage to stay safe — line-buffered tools can still hold frames otherwise.

Diff stat: 1 file, +12 / -0. Documentation-only.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Sanitized issue evidence — the issue describes that a custom stdio bridge wrapper using a large blocking `read(N)` produced `imsg rpc timeout (chats.list)` until the wrapper was changed to forward bytes/lines as soon as they were available. The fix documents this constraint at the spot operators actually look.

- **Real environment tested:** Local Node 22.x. Probe at `/tmp/probe_84330.mjs` (a) verifies the `<Warning>` block was added to `docs/channels/imessage.md` and lives inside the "Remote Mac over SSH" Tab, (b) checks coverage against the issue's "Expected Documentation" list — all six bullets present (forward stdin/stdout promptly, preserve newlines, don't wait for EOF, avoid fixed-size blocking reads, keep stderr separate), (c) confirms the exact symptom phrasing `imsg rpc timeout` is cited so operators searching for that error find this section, (d) confirms surrounding sections (Tab intro + Requirements heading) are unchanged, (e) checks `<Warning>` open/close tags are balanced.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `node /tmp/probe_84330.mjs`

- **Evidence after fix:**

```
PASS: <Warning> block added that mentions cliPath wrapper
PASS: warning block lives inside the 'Remote Mac over SSH' Tab
PASS: all 6 required bullets from issue's "Expected Documentation" are covered
PASS: warning cites the exact symptom from the issue ('imsg rpc timeout')
PASS: surrounding sections (Tab intro + Requirements heading) are unchanged
PASS: <Warning> open/close tags balanced (4 pairs)

ALL CASES PASS
```

- **Observed result after fix:** Operators following the "Remote Mac over SSH" Tab now see the streaming requirement directly under the wrapper-script example. The Warning calls out the visible symptom (`imsg rpc timeout`), the structural requirements, and a common pitfall (`grep` pipelines without `stdbuf -oL`) that produces the same failure mode.

- **What was not tested:** No iMessage instance was actually run against a buffering wrapper to reproduce the symptom — this is a documentation-only change to an existing operator-facing manual page. The doc updates the section the issue explicitly suggests in "Suggested Location" (`docs/channels/imessage.md` near the Remote Mac over SSH wrapper section).

## Audit (per CLAUDE rules — all 5 steps)

- **Existing-helper check:** No code helper introduced — documentation block additive. The `<Warning>` Mintlify component is the established callout convention used throughout this doc and across the docs site. PASS
- **Shared-helper caller check:** No code is being modified, so no caller-side contracts exist. PASS
- **Broader-fix rival scan:** `gh pr list --search '84330 in:title,body'` and `gh pr list --search 'iMessage cliPath wrapper'` return no open PRs. Issue timeline shows zero cross-references. PASS
- **Recent-merge audit:** `git log --oneline -5 -- docs/channels/imessage.md` shows no recent commits touching this file. PASS
- **Prototype-pollution scan:** N/A — markdown.

target.title: perf(infra): cache sync JSON reads with stat validation
target.body:
## Summary

Sync JSON reads dominate TUI cold start — profiled startup made ~210K `tryReadJsonSync` calls against ~380 unique paths, with each bundled plugin's `package.json` re-read ~1500 times. This adds a process-scoped, stat-validated memo as an **opt-in** fast path and leaves the legacy uncached API alone.

- **New cached entrypoints** in `src/infra/json-files.ts`: `tryReadJsonSync`, `readRootJsonObjectSync`. Each call does one `statSync` and returns the cached parse when `(mtime, size, inode)` matches. Atomic writes, external edits, and deletions evict naturally.
- **Legacy `loadJsonFile` (`src/infra/json-file.ts`) bypasses the cache.** All its callers — subagent registry store (own stat-signature cache), `cli-credentials`, `auth-profiles/*`, `current-conversation-bindings`, `doctor-auth-*` — either run their own invalidation or are auth/transient state where a stat-validated cache hit across re-auth would be wrong.
- TUI startup hot paths can opt in by importing directly from `json-files.ts`.

Disable globally with `OPENCLAW_DISABLE_JSON_READ_CACHE=1` or call `clearJsonReadCache()`.

## Behavior addressed

TUI cold start spent 20–30s in 200K+ sync JSON reads hammering the same plugin manifests. After this, each unique file is read+parsed once; subsequent calls cost a single `statSync` (~5–10µs) instead of open + read + parse (~50–100µs).

## Real environment tested

macOS, Node 24, `pnpm openclaw` cold launch.

## Exact steps or command run after this patch

```
pnpm build
pnpm openclaw
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/session-utils.subagent.test.ts src/infra/json-file.test.ts src/infra/json-files.test.ts
```

## Evidence after fix

- TUI responsive in single-digit seconds vs. 20–30s freeze on `main`.
- Vitest green: 81 tests in `session-utils.subagent.test.ts` (including the "reuses one subagent registry disk snapshot" invariant) + 23 tests across `json-file(s)`.

## What was not tested

- Long-running session soak around atomic writes / external edits.
- Crabbox / Testbox broader suites (CI on PR).

## Trade-off

`src/plugins/CLAUDE.md` cautions against fs-primitive metadata caches. By keeping the cache opt-in and leaving `loadJsonFile` callers uncached, the surface area is narrow: auth/credential and registry paths are unaffected, and the cache only fires where TUI startup actually benefits. The structural fix is to memoize at the snapshot/registry layer — that work continues in #84283 / #84302 / #84339. This PR is the pragmatic floor.

target.title: feat(web-fetch): add allowPrivateNetwork config for web_fetch
target.body:
## Summary

- Add opt-in `tools.web.fetch.allowPrivateNetwork` config key (boolean, default `false`) so `web_fetch` can reach private/internal network addresses (localhost, 10.x, 192.168.x, 172.16-31.x) when explicitly enabled.
- Default behavior is unchanged: private networks remain blocked unless the operator explicitly opts in.

## Problem

`web_fetch` blocks all private/internal network addresses via the SSRF guard. There is no config-level way to opt in to private network access, blocking agent architectures where agents need to call local services:

```
[security] blocked URL fetch (url-fetch) target=http://127.0.0.1:9090/api/...
reason=Blocked hostname or private/internal/special-use IP address
```

The `ToolsWebFetchSchema` uses `.strict()`, so users cannot work around this without an upstream schema change.

## Changes

- **`src/infra/net/ssrf.ts`**: Add optional `options.allowPrivateNetwork` parameter to `resolvePinnedHostname()`. When `true`, skips blocked-hostname and private-IP checks; pins private IP literals directly without DNS lookup.
- **`src/agents/tools/web-fetch.ts`**: Add `resolveFetchAllowPrivateNetwork()` resolver (defaults `false`). Thread `allowPrivateNetwork` through `fetchWithRedirects` and `runWebFetch` into `resolvePinnedHostname`.
- **`src/config/zod-schema.agent-runtime.ts`**: Add `allowPrivateNetwork: z.boolean().optional()` to `ToolsWebFetchSchema`.
- **`src/config/types.tools.ts`**: Add `allowPrivateNetwork?: boolean` to the fetch config type.
- **`src/config/schema.ts`**: Add label and help text for the new config key.
- **`src/agents/tools/web-fetch.ssrf.test.ts`**: Add two tests (allow private when enabled; block private when explicitly false). Fix mock to forward the new `options` parameter.
- **`CHANGELOG.md`**: Add entry referencing #39604.

## User-visible changes

New config key:
```json5
"tools": {
  "web": {
    "fetch": { "allowPrivateNetwork": true }
  }
}
```
When `true`, `web_fetch` can reach private/internal network addresses. Default remains `false` (no behavior change for existing users).

## Security impact

- **New capability**: opt-in only, default `false`.
- **Risk**: operators enabling this in untrusted environments could expose internal services.
- **Mitigation**: explicit config required; secure default; same trust model as operator-controlled gateway config.

## Testing performed

- `pnpm lint` -- 0 warnings, 0 errors
- `pnpm build` -- clean
- `pnpm test` -- 5947 passed, 2 failed (pre-existing lobster subprocess timeout, unrelated)
- SSRF test suite: 7/7 passed (including 2 new tests for the `allowPrivateNetwork` path)

Closes #39604

target.title: fix: reload config in slash command routing so dmScope is respected
target.body:
Fixes #39605

## Problem

Native slash commands (`/status`, `/ask`, etc.) in Discord, Telegram, and Slack DMs route to `agent:main:main` instead of respecting `session.dmScope` (e.g. `per-channel-peer`). Normal text messages in the same conversation correctly route to the expected session key like `agent:main:discord:direct:<userId>`.

## Root Cause

All three channel providers load config once at startup and capture it in a closure. The message handlers already call `loadConfig()` fresh per message (e.g. `message-handler.preflight.ts`), but the slash/native-command routing paths reuse the stale startup snapshot. Since `resolveAgentRoute` reads `cfg.session.dmScope` to compute the session key, slash commands always use the config state from startup time.

## Fix

Call `loadConfig()` in each channel's slash/native-command routing section before route resolution, matching the pattern already used by message handlers.

### Changes

- **`src/discord/monitor/native-command.ts`**: Load fresh config in `dispatchDiscordCommandInteraction` before routing; promote `loadConfig` from type-only to value import
- **`src/telegram/bot-native-commands.ts`**: Load fresh config in `resolveCommandRuntimeContext` before routing; add `loadConfig` value import
- **`src/slack/monitor/slash.ts`**: Load fresh config in `handleSlashCommand` before routing; add `loadConfig` import

### Testing

- Type-check clean (`pnpm tsgo`)
- Lint clean (`pnpm lint` — only pre-existing `debug-route.ts` error)
- All routing tests pass (`src/routing/resolve-route.test.ts`, `src/routing/session-key.test.ts`)

target.title: config: Support setting userTimezone per agent
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: OpenClaw only supported a single global `agents.defaults.userTimezone`, so multi-agent setups could not give different agents correct local time context.
- Why it matters: Agent-local time affects system prompt time context, heartbeat active-hours, session status, envelope timestamps, system event timestamps, and time-based helper prompts like memory flush/post-compaction context.
- What changed: Added `agents.list[].userTimezone`, introduced shared agent-aware timezone resolution, threaded `agentId` through the relevant call sites, validated configured IANA timezones in schema, and added tests/docs for the new behavior.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): This does not change `timeFormat` per agent, host timezone fallback behavior, or explicit non-`user` timezone settings such as fixed IANA/UTC/local envelope config.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #39579 

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- New optional config: `agents.list[].userTimezone`.
- When set, the active agent's `userTimezone` now overrides `agents.defaults.userTimezone`.
- This per-agent timezone is now used for:
  - system prompt time context
  - `session_status` time line
  - heartbeat `activeHours.timezone: "user"`
  - `"user"` envelope timestamps
  - `"user"` system-event timestamps
  - cron-style current-time injection used by memory flush and post-compaction context
- If no per-agent value is set, behavior remains: `agents.defaults.userTimezone` then host timezone fallback.
- Config validation now rejects invalid IANA timezone strings for both default and per-agent `userTimezone`.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: podman
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): gateway + multi-agent config; verified via user testing
- Relevant config (redacted):
  ```json5
  {
    agents: {
      defaults: {
        userTimezone: "America/New_York",
        envelopeTimezone: "user",
      },
      list: [
        {
          id: "work",
          userTimezone: "America/Los_Angeles",
        },
      ],
    },
  }
  ```

### Steps

1. Configure `agents.defaults.userTimezone` and a different `agents.list[].userTimezone` for a non-default agent.
2. Run flows using that agent, including session status, prompt/time injection paths, and any envelope/system-event path using `"user"` timezone.
3. Observe the active agent uses its own timezone instead of the global default.

### Expected

- The active agent consistently uses its own `userTimezone` override everywhere `"user"` timezone semantics are intended.

### Actual

- Before this change, the default/global timezone was used even for non-default agents.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - Multi-agent config with different default/per-agent timezones
  - User-visible behavior manually tested as a user
  - Full local validation: `pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test`
- Edge cases checked:
  - Agent without override falls back to `agents.defaults.userTimezone`
  - No configured timezone falls back to host timezone
  - Invalid configured timezones are rejected by schema validation
  - Envelope/system-event `"user"` timezone paths receive the active `agentId`
- What you did **not** verify:
  - Every external channel manually end-to-end in a live environment

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:
  - Optional only: add `agents.list[].userTimezone` where agent-specific timezone behavior is desired.

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly:
  - Remove per-agent `userTimezone` entries and rely on `agents.defaults.userTimezone` or revert this PR.
- Files/config to restore:
  - Agent timezone config in `agents.list[]`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for:
  - Non-default agents showing the default timezone in prompts, envelopes, heartbeat windows, or status output
  - `"user"` timezone behavior not varying by agent

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: A call site using `"user"` timezone semantics could miss passing `agentId` and silently fall back to the default timezone.
  - Mitigation: Updated the shared helpers/call sites across gateway, envelope, heartbeat, memory flush, post-compaction context, and system events; added targeted tests.
- Risk: Rejecting invalid timezone strings at schema level could break previously accepted but incorrect configs.
  - Mitigation: This is intentional fail-fast validation; docs and tests cover the accepted IANA-only behavior.

## AI Assistance

This PR was AI-assisted using GPT-5.4 and Gemini 3.1 Pro.

CLI-side validation improves user feedback and fails earlier with a clearer message.

target.title: fix: guard llm_output hook against undefined lastAssistant after timeout
target.body:
## Summary

- When an LLM request times out before producing any assistant output, `lastAssistant` is `undefined`. The `llm_output` hook was still fired in this case, causing plugin handlers to crash with "Cannot convert undefined or null to object" when they try to enumerate properties of the undefined value.
- Added a null guard: the `llm_output` hook is now only called when `lastAssistant` is defined (i.e., the LLM actually produced output). Also added a `?? undefined` fallback for `getUsageTotals()` to ensure the usage field is cleanly `undefined` rather than potentially `null`.

- [ ] Verify normal (non-timeout) LLM responses still trigger the `llm_output` hook as before
- [ ] `pnpm tsgo` passes
- [ ] `pnpm test` passes
