target.title: docs: Clarify Main Session Cron behavior and HEARTBEAT.md integration
target.body:
## Problem

When using Main Session Cron jobs (`--session main --system-event`), the cron event prompt does not include the "Read HEARTBEAT.md" instruction that the default heartbeat prompt contains.

This causes confusion for users who expect their HEARTBEAT.md tasks to be executed when cron events trigger.

**Related Issues:**
- #11726 - Cron jobs with sessionTarget: "main" inject systemEvent but dont trigger agent turn
- #10538 - Cron main-session jobs frequently "skipped: timeout waiting for main lane to become idle"

## Root Cause

In `/app/src/infra/heartbeat-runner.ts`, when cron events are present, the system calls `buildCronEventPrompt()` instead of the default `HEARTBEAT_PROMPT`:

```typescript
const prompt = hasExecCompletion
  ? buildExecEventPrompt({ deliverToUser: params.canRelayToUser })
  : hasCronEvents
    ? buildCronEventPrompt(cronEvents, { deliverToUser: params.canRelayToUser })  // ← No "Read HEARTBEAT.md"
    : resolveHeartbeatPrompt(params.cfg, params.heartbeat);  // ← Contains "Read HEARTBEAT.md"
```

## Changes

1. **`/app/src/infra/heartbeat-events-filter.ts`** - Added code comment explaining the behavior and workaround
2. **`/app/docs/automation/cron-jobs.md`** - Added "HEARTBEAT.md Integration" section with workaround guidance
3. **`/app/docs/reference/templates/AGENTS.md`** - Added "Handling Cron System Events" section to template

## Workaround for Users

Until this is fixed, users can add the following to their `AGENTS.md`:

```markdown
## Cron System Event Handling

When you receive a cron system event (prompt contains "A scheduled reminder has been triggered"):
1. Read HEARTBEAT.md to find the task configuration for this event
2. Execute the task (e.g., send daily brief, wake-up call, etc.)
3. Report completion to the user
```

## Future Work

Consider adding a config option to include HEARTBEAT.md instruction in cron event prompts, or merge cron event handling with heartbeat prompt logic for consistency.

target.title: feat(feishu): comprehensive plugin enhancements — streaming, dedup, skills, calendar, and stability
target.body:
## Summary

This PR consolidates all Feishu plugin enhancements developed across multiple iterations (supersedes #38770, #38772, #39409, #39440). All previous PRs were closed without merging; this PR contains the complete changeset.

---

## Issues Fixed

### Streaming Card Display

1. **Text Duplication in Streaming Cards**: `mergeStreamingText()` fallback path duplicated cumulative text → bypass it, treat `onPartialReply` text as complete state
2. **Content Duplication Across Cards**: each card showed cumulative text instead of its own block → track `blockOffset`, slice cumulative text per card
3. **Content Disappearing on Card Close**: `closeStreaming()` used per-block text while card displayed cumulative text → `streamText` consistently uses sliced card text
4. **Missing Tail Text After Last Block**: `deliver(kind=final)` sent full duplicate or was skipped → extract only undelivered tail `text.slice(blockOffset)`, send if non-empty
5. **Extra Streaming Card After Last Block**: `onPartialReply` kept creating new cards → only start new card if substantial new content beyond `blockOffset`
6. **Slow Streaming Updates**: `await this.queue` blocked `onPartialReply` on API round-trips; no flush timer → remove `await` (fire-and-forget), add `pendingFlushTimer` (300ms auto-flush)
7. **Markdown Crash Guard**: `isSafeToRender()` blocks updates during unclosed code fences
8. **Typing Indicator Emoji**: Changed from `Typing` to `Get` (fix reaction 231001 error)
9. **Broken CardKit Streaming**: Replaced broken CardKit chunked patching with Semantic Block Delivery using `block` events from `reply-dispatcher.ts`

### Message Deduplication (3-Layer)

10. **event_id Dedup**: Lark SDK `EventDispatcher.parse()` flattens header into callback data — `event_id` IS available. Replaced content-hash workaround with proper `event_id` dedup as recommended by Feishu docs
11. **Stale Message Filtering in Debounce**: When debounce batch has messages with >2min time gap, drop older messages and record their IDs in persistent dedup
12. **create_time-based Stale Rejection**: Feishu retries re-deliver messages with new `message_id`/`event_id` but same `create_time`. Track last processed `create_time` per `chat+sender` and reject stale duplicates

### Message Control & Abort

13. **Fast Abort Bypass**: Trigger words (`stop`, `停止`, `取消`, `esc`, etc.) skip 2s debounce + bypass `chatQueue` → direct dispatch to `tryFastAbortFromMessage`
14. **Message Recall Abort**: Register `im.message.recalled_v1` → clear debounce buffer, record in dedup, synthesize `/stop` command via `dispatchFeishuMessageDirect`

### Feishu Ops Tools (Skills)

15. **feishu_contacts**: Search contacts with auto-sync from API
16. **feishu_groups**: List/search bot groups with auto-sync
17. **feishu_send**: Send text/file/@all to users or groups
18. **feishu_calendar**: Create, **update**, and **delete** calendar events by `event_id`
19. **2-Tier Contact Sync**: When list API returns 0 results, fallback to BFS department traversal + per-department user fetch (ported from `feishu-ops/scripts/update-contacts.py`)
20. **SKILL.md Rewrite**: All skill docs converted to English with JSON tool-call examples

### Code Quality

21. **Error Messages Standardized to English** across all new modules
22. **Group Search Hint**: `searchGroupsOrSync()` lists all bot groups and prompts user to add bot to target group if not found
23. **Mention Logic**: More resilient verify mention logic and fix log propagation
24. **SDK v1.30+ Response Parsing**: Fix `proactive-send.ts` to handle Lark SDK top-level response format (`{ file_key }` instead of `{ code: 0, data: { file_key } }`)

### Other

25. **Daemon Working Directory**: Resolve gateway working directory to installation root

---

## Files Changed

| File | Changes |
|---|---|
| `extensions/feishu/src/bot.ts` | event_id dedup, create_time dedup, fast abort bypass, recall handler, mention logic |
| `extensions/feishu/src/dedup.ts` | `checkAndRecordCreateTime()`, create_time tracking per chat+sender |
| `extensions/feishu/src/reply-dispatcher.ts` | Block-boundary card splitting, blockOffset tracking, tail text, semantic block delivery |
| `extensions/feishu/src/streaming-card.ts` | Remove mergeStreamingText, async queue, pendingFlushTimer, isSafeToRender |
| `extensions/feishu/src/typing.ts` | Change typing emoji to Get |
| `extensions/feishu/src/inbound-debounce.ts` | Stale message filtering, removeFromBuffer() |
| `extensions/feishu/src/feishu-ops-tools.ts` | Register contacts, groups, send, calendar tools |
| `extensions/feishu/src/contacts.ts` | 2-tier sync with department fallback |
| `extensions/feishu/src/calendar.ts` | Update and delete calendar events |
| `extensions/feishu/src/proactive-send.ts` | SDK v1.30+ response parsing fix |
| `extensions/feishu/skills/*/SKILL.md` | English rewrite with JSON examples |
| `src/daemon/program-args.ts` | Working directory resolution fix |

## Supersedes

- #38770 (CLOSED — streaming card fix)
- #38772 (CLOSED — streaming + event_id dedup)
- #39409 (CLOSED — message control, skills, stability)
- #39440 (CLOSED — calendar update/delete)

target.title: fix(feishu): report connection/event activity for health monitor
target.body:
## Summary

Implement Feishu channel health-signal reporting so gateway health monitoring can observe runtime activity correctly:

- add a status sink path from Feishu channel startup into monitor runtime updates
- report lifecycle connectivity (`connected`) and mode (`websocket`/`webhook`)
- report `lastEventAt` on inbound Feishu events
- clear connectivity on shutdown/error paths

This improves health monitor observability for Feishu channels and reduces false unhealthy restarts caused by missing runtime activity signals.

## Changes

- `extensions/feishu/src/channel.ts`
  - pass `statusSink` into `monitorFeishuProvider` (`ctx.setStatus` patch sink)
- `extensions/feishu/src/monitor.ts`
  - plumb `statusSink` through monitor options to single-account monitors
- `extensions/feishu/src/monitor.account.ts`
  - accept `statusSink`
  - mark inbound events as activity (`lastEventAt`) and connected
  - initialize mode/connected status for account monitor lifecycle
- `extensions/feishu/src/monitor.transport.ts`
  - accept `statusSink`
  - websocket/webhook start/abort/error paths update connectivity/error status
  - webhook request ingress updates `lastEventAt`

## Issue

Fixes #39488

target.title: fix(routing): map discord account IDs to matching agents
target.body:
## Summary
- add Discord-only fallback routing: when no binding matches, map `accountId` to an agent with the same ID
- keep fallback disabled for the `default` account id to avoid surprising overrides
- add regression tests for positive auto-match and default-account guard

## Why
In multi-account Discord setups, account names are commonly aligned with agent IDs. Without explicit bindings, traffic incorrectly fell back to the default agent, which could bypass intended per-agent permission boundaries.

## Behavior
When `channel=discord` and no explicit binding matches:
- if `accountId` matches an existing agent id (case-insensitive), route to that agent
- if `accountId=default`, keep existing default-agent behavior

## Test evidence
- `pnpm vitest run --config vitest.unit.config.ts src/routing/resolve-route.test.ts`
  - Passed: 1 file, 43 tests

Fixes #39428

target.title: fix(gateway): forward child session node events to spawnedBy subscribers
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: gateway node subscriptions are keyed by exact `sessionKey`, so child-session node events disappear when only the parent session is subscribed.
- Why it matters: spawned agent chat activity can look dead from the gateway/node subscriber side even while work is actually running.
- What changed: added a direct-miss fallback in `createNodeSubscriptionManager.sendToSession()` that walks `spawnedBy` lineage, canonicalizes ancestor keys, and routes to the first ancestor with subscribers; added a focused regression test and changelog entry.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this does not change ACP execution, session transcript bridging, or broader subagent progress/status surfaces.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #34852
- Related #34905

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Spawned child-session node events now reach parent-session node subscribers when only the parent session is subscribed.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local dev checkout
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): gateway node subscriptions
- Relevant config (redacted): default test harness

### Steps

1. Subscribe a node to a parent session key.

target.title: Doctor: structure gateway service repairs
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: after #83753, #84290, and #84326, the gateway-services doctor bucket still had legacy positional service scan/config repair paths, so structured lint/fix/dry-run coverage stopped before gateway service drift.
- Solution: convert extra gateway-like service detection and gateway service config drift into structured health checks while preserving the existing positional doctor order and service-repair policies.
- Rigor/idempotency: structured detection is read-only; real repair still delegates to the existing gateway service helpers; dry-run emits service effects without mutating config or supervisor state; active systemd and externally managed service policy remain explicit operator warnings.
- What did NOT change: this does not add public CLI flags, change gateway install defaults, or make externally managed/system-scoped service cleanup automatic.

This PR is intentionally stacked on #83753, #84290, and #84326. The diff against `main` includes those earlier stages; the new gateway-services stage starts at `cb7536ccae fix(doctor): structure gateway service repairs`.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #83753
- Related #84290
- Related #84326
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior addressed: Structured doctor repair conversion for gateway extra-service detection and gateway service config drift, with dry-run service effects and preserved service-repair policy behavior.
- Real environment tested: Local WSL source checkout on the stacked branch.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/commands/doctor-gateway-services.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.test.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts src/flows/health-checks.ts`; `node node_modules/@typescript/native-preview/bin/tsgo.js --noEmit --pretty false -p tsconfig.json`; `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/commands/doctor-gateway-services.test.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.test.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts src/flows/doctor-health-conversion-plan.test.ts src/flows/doctor-repair-flow.test.ts --reporter=dot`; `git diff --check`; `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/flows/doctor-repair-flow.test.ts --reporter=dot`; `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/flows/doctor-repair-flow.test.ts`; `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/flows/doctor-core-checks.test.ts --reporter=dot`; `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/flows/doctor-core-checks.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.test.ts src/flows/doctor-repair-flow.test.ts`; `/mnt/c/src/claws-hapi/.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local`.
- Evidence after fix: format check passed; tsgo passed; focused tests passed 5 files / 87 tests; added effect-only detect-after regression passed 1 file / 7 tests; added per-check detect-after coverage passed 1 file / 26 tests; focused format check passed; diff check passed; autoreview returned clean with no accepted/actionable findings after two accepted findings were fixed.
- Observed result after fix: gateway-services now registers `core/doctor/gateway-services/extra` and `core/doctor/gateway-services/config`; dry-run reports service effects; active systemd command/entrypoint rewrite blocking is visible as a structured finding; dry-run skips unrepairable legacy service cleanup instead of promising removals the real path cannot perform; legacy repair helpers are not called during structured dry-run.
- What was not tested: full changed gate, live systemd/launchd service mutation, actual gateway service reinstall/stage on a host supervisor, package/update survivor lanes, and Crabbox/Testbox remote proof.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): prior behavior kept extra gateway service scanning and gateway service config drift on legacy positional helper paths rather than first-class structured health checks with dry-run/diff effects.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: the previous structured doctor stages had not yet converted the gateway-services bucket, leaving extra-service scan and service config repair outside the same structured repair contract.
- Missing detection / guardrail: no focused tests asserted structured gateway service config dry-run effects, active systemd rewrite-block findings, or dry-run parity for unrepairable legacy services.
- Contributing context (if known): #83753 established the structured maintenance pattern, #84290 continued plugin install/registry repairs, and #84326 converted sandbox checks.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/commands/doctor-gateway-services.test.ts`, `src/flows/doctor-core-checks.test.ts`, `src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts`, `src/flows/doctor-health-conversion-plan.test.ts`, `src/flows/doctor-repair-flow.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: structured lint/repair/dry-run behavior for extra gateway-like services and gateway service config drift.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: these are doctor/gateway-service seam behaviors; focused tests cover detector output, service effects, positional ordering, policy skips, and legacy repair delegation without requiring a live system supervisor.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): `src/commands/doctor-gateway-services.test.ts` covers legacy helper behavior; `src/flows/doctor-repair-flow.test.ts` covers shared repair orchestration.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Doctor lint/fix can now report gateway extra-service and gateway service config findings through the structured health-check path while preserving existing repair order, prompts, and service safety rules.

## Dry-run Diff / Effects Catalog

This PR catalogs gateway service repairs as service effects, not file diffs. The service helper owns supervisor metadata, so dry-run reports exactly which service action would run and leaves blocked/unrepairable cases as findings or warnings.

- `core/doctor/gateway-services/extra`
  - Service effect: `kind: "service"`, action `would-remove-legacy-gateway-service`, target the legacy service label, `dryRunSafe: false`.
  - Blocked system-scoped, external, or unrepairable service cleanup is reported as a dry-run warning/finding rather than an effect, so the plan does not promise a mutation doctor will skip.

- `core/doctor/gateway-services/config`
  - Service effect: `kind: "service"`, action `would-update-gateway-service-config`, target `openclaw-gateway`, `dryRunSafe: false`.
  - Active systemd rewrites that are blocked by platform/service ownership remain findings and warnings. No fake diff is emitted because the install/stage repair updates supervisor state through the service helper rather than a local config object available to doctor.

- `core/doctor/gateway-services/platform-notes`
  - Platform note checks stay read-only. They catalog current/shipped behavior but have no repair effect or dry-run diff.

## Repair Validation / Detect-After

Invariant for all real structured repairs in this bucket: a successful non-dry-run repair is not considered fixed from its effect record alone. The shared repair runner immediately re-runs the same health check, scoped to the original finding paths, and records remaining findings if the install, cleanup, rewrite, or state update is still detectable. Dry-run remains preview-only because no mutation is applied. This PR adds an explicit regression test for effect-only repairs so service/package/process effects without config diffs still get detect-after validation, plus per-check tests covering the converted manifest, registry, sandbox, and gateway repair buckets.

- Legacy gateway service cleanup runs through the gateway service scanner/removal path, then `core/doctor/gateway-services/extra` detection must no longer report the legacy service.
- Gateway service config repair runs through `maybeRepairGatewayServiceConfig`, then `core/doctor/gateway-services/config` detection must no longer report service drift.
- Blocked active systemd rewrites and unrepairable system/external services return `skipped`; skipped repairs are not counted as fixed and are intentionally not validated as repaired.

## Stack Refresh / Detect-After Test Placement

- Branch restacked on the updated sandbox branch and pushed with gateway-owned detect-after proof: `2cd30e3191 test(doctor): validate gateway repair detection`.
- Bucket covered here: `core/doctor/gateway-services/extra` and `core/doctor/gateway-services/config` detect-after validation. Real gateway repairs now return matching real service effects, so dry-run and applied-repair effect accounting align.
- Exact focused proof after restack: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/commands/doctor-gateway-services.test.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.test.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts src/flows/doctor-health-conversion-plan.test.ts src/flows/doctor-repair-flow.test.ts --reporter=dot` passed 5 files / 94 tests; `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/commands/doctor-gateway-services.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.test.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts src/flows/doctor-repair-flow.test.ts src/flows/health-checks.ts` passed; `git diff --check` passed.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: WSL local checkout
- Runtime/container: Node v24.15.0 local source checkout
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): doctor CLI/gateway service checks
- Relevant config (redacted): synthetic gateway service states for extra legacy services, port drift, active systemd rewrite block, external repair policy, and unrepairable system-scoped legacy services

### Steps

1. Run focused gateway service and doctor repair tests.
2. Run format, diff, tsgo, and autoreview.
3. Verify accepted autoreview findings are fixed and covered by tests.

### Expected

- Structured gateway checks report service drift and extra services, preserve legacy repair behavior, and do not overpromise dry-run mutations.

### Actual

- Expected behavior observed in focused local tests and autoreview clean closeout.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: local format, diff check, tsgo, focused tests, and autoreview.
- Edge cases checked: wrapper-managed services remain aligned; source-checkout warnings are not duplicated; active systemd rewrites become structured findings; external service repair policy skips cleanup; dry-run skips unrepairable legacy service cleanup; structured repair runs at the gateway-services contribution position.
- What you did **not** verify: full changed gate, live supervisor mutation, actual gateway reinstall/stage, package/update survivor lanes, or remote Crabbox/Testbox proof.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.

- Risk: dry-run could promise service cleanup that real repair would skip.

target.title: fix(spawn): allow mode="session" without thread=true for headless persistent sessions
target.body:
## Summary

- Removes the guard that blocked `mode="session"` when `thread=true` was not set in both `sessions_spawn` (subagent) and ACP spawn paths
- Enables headless persistent sub-agent sessions on **all channels** (Telegram, Slack, WhatsApp, Signal, etc.), not just Discord
- Thread binding remains optional — when `thread=true` is passed, it still attempts platform thread binding as before

## Problem

`sessions_spawn` with `mode: "session"` was hardcoded to require `thread: true`. Since thread binding currently only works on Discord (via `subagent_spawning` hooks), persistent sub-agent sessions were completely unavailable on all other channels. This blocked the orchestrator pattern (main → orchestrator → workers) documented in the official docs from working on non-Discord channels.

## Fix

The guard clause `if (spawnMode === "session" && !requestThreadBinding)` is removed from both `subagent-spawn.ts` and `acp-spawn.ts`. Session persistence and thread binding are independent concepts:

1. **Session persistence** (`mode: "session"`) — session stays alive to receive announces and `sessions_send`
2. **Thread binding** (`thread: true`) — routes platform thread I/O to the session (Discord/Slack feature)

The rest of the code already handles the `thread=false` case correctly — thread binding is only attempted `if (requestThreadBinding)` at line 449.

Fixes #23414

## Test plan

- [x] Updated existing test to verify `mode="session"` without `thread=true` proceeds without error
- [x] All 9 spawn hooks tests pass
- [ ] Manual: spawn sub-agent with `mode: "session"` on Telegram — should succeed
- [ ] Manual: spawn sub-agent with `mode: "session", thread: true` on Discord — should still work as before

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: fix(gateway): suppress NO_REPLY lead fragment in chat final message
target.body:
## Summary

- Fix `NO_REPLY` partial fragment ("NO") leaking into webchat UI when the final chat event is emitted
- The delta throttle correctly suppresses lead fragments via `isSilentReplyLeadFragment`, but the final message emission only checked `isSilentReplyText` (exact token match) — a partial buffer like "NO" passed through as a visible final message
- Add `isSilentReplyPrefixText` check to the final message guard, which correctly distinguishes uppercase-only protocol fragments ("NO", "NO_") from legitimate short replies ("No", "No problem")

Closes #39335

## Test plan

- [x] Added test: partial "NO" in buffer at lifecycle end → final message is `undefined`
- [x] Existing test: mixed-case "No" reply is preserved in final

target.title: feat(pairing): add owner notification on new pairing requests
target.body:
## Summary
Adds secure owner notifications when new pairing requests arrive. Notifications bypass the AI agent context entirely to prevent injection attacks from untrusted requester data.

## Changes
- Added `pairing.notify` config section with target, channel, accountId options
- Added `pairing:request` internal hook fired on new pairing requests
- Added `notify-owner.ts` with sanitization and multi-channel send support
- Sanitizes all untrusted fields (control chars, zero-width, bidi, length limits)
- Supports: iMessage, Telegram, WhatsApp, Discord, Signal, Slack

## Configuration
```yaml
pairing:
  notify:
    enabled: true
    target: '+14155551234'
    channel: imessage
```

## Security Design
- Notification goes directly to owner via configured channel — never enters AI agent context

## Summary

- **Problem:** Some OpenAI-compatible providers (e.g. Kimi) send tool call IDs like `functions.exec:0` as `functions exec:0` (space instead of dot) and tool names like `exec` as `functions exec`. Tool result matching fails, causing "Tool functions exec not found" errors.
- **Why it matters:** Users on Kimi and similar providers cannot complete tool-using flows; matching is done by ID and name, and the mangled forms did not match.
- **What changed:** Added `normalizeMangledToolCallId()` in `tool-call-id.ts` to rewrite `functions ` -> `functions.` in IDs; use it in `extractToolResultId()` and as the key in `sanitizeToolCallIdsForCloudCodeAssist()` so mangled and canonical IDs map to the same sanitized value. Added `normalizePrefixedToolName()` in `session-transcript-repair.ts` to strip `functions.` / `functions ` from tool names; applied in assistant block handling, `hasToolCallName`, and `normalizeToolResultName()` so tool-result names are normalized and pairing works.

target.title: feat(exec): add bubblewrap namespace sandbox for safeBins commands
target.body:
## What

Adds kernel-level filesystem isolation for safeBins-approved commands using [bubblewrap](https://github.com/containers/bubblewrap) (bwrap) unprivileged user namespaces on Linux.

## Why

The current safeBins fence validates command names but can't prevent absolute-path bypasses (e.g. `/usr/bin/rm`). With bwrap, the binary simply doesn't exist inside the sandbox — filesystem isolation is enforced by the kernel, not by argument parsing.

## How

When `tools.exec.nsSandbox.mode` is set to `"bwrap"`, commands that pass safeBins validation are wrapped in a bwrap namespace where only:

- **Approved binaries** — resolved from `trustedSafeBinDirs`, mounted read-only
- **Shell binaries** — `sh`, `bash`, `env` always available for `sh -c`
- **System libraries** — `/lib`, `/usr/lib` etc. for dynamic linking (read-only)
- **System config** — SSL certs, DNS, locale (read-only)
- **Working directory** — mounted read-write
- **Extra mounts** — configurable via `nsSandbox.extraBinds`

Everything else is invisible. Namespace isolation uses `--unshare-all --share-net --die-with-parent`.

### Interaction with other security layers

| State | Behavior |
|---|---|
| Trust window active | No sandbox (run directly) |
| No trust + safeBins match + bwrap enabled | Wrapped in bwrap namespace |
| No trust + safeBins match + bwrap disabled | Run directly (existing behavior) |
| No trust + not safeBins | Ask for approval (existing behavior) |
| Docker sandbox (`host=sandbox`) | Docker takes precedence, no bwrap |
| PTY requested | Skip bwrap (terminal allocation unreliable in namespaces) |

Composes cleanly with trust windows (PR #39126).

### Config

```jsonc
{
  "tools": {
    "exec": {
      "nsSandbox": {
        "mode": "bwrap",  // "bwrap" | "none" (default)
        "extraBinds": [
          { "src": "/opt/data", "writable": false },
          { "src": "/opt/output", "writable": true }
        ]
      }
    }
  }
}
```

Requires bubblewrap installed (`bwrap`). Ships with Fedora/Ubuntu via Flatpak deps. Falls back to no-sandbox gracefully if not available.

## Files changed (8)

| File | Change |
|---|---|
| `src/infra/exec-bwrap-sandbox.ts` | **New** — Core module: bwrap arg builder, detection, config normalization |
| `src/infra/exec-bwrap-sandbox.test.ts` | **New** — 18 unit tests |
| `src/config/types.tools.ts` | Add `nsSandbox` to `ExecToolConfig` |
| `src/agents/bash-tools.exec-types.ts` | Add `nsSandbox` to `ExecToolDefaults` |
| `src/agents/bash-tools.exec-runtime.ts` | Add bwrap spawn path in `runExecProcess` |
| `src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-gateway.ts` | Build bwrap params when safeBins match |
| `src/agents/bash-tools.exec.ts` | Thread bwrap params from gateway → runtime |
| `src/agents/pi-tools.ts` | Thread `nsSandbox` config through defaults |

## Testing

- 18 new unit tests for bwrap module (arg building, mount dedup, config normalization, binary resolution)
- 47 existing safeBins tests still passing
- `tsc --noEmit` — zero errors from our changes
- Manual: bwrap v0.11.0 on Fedora 43

## AI Disclosure

AI-assisted (Tommy/Claude Opus 4.6). All code reviewed and understood by contributor.

target.title: feat(agents): add opt-in CaMeL prompt injection defense
target.body:
## Summary

Adds opt-in CaMeL (CApabilities for MachinE Learning) prompt injection defense to OpenClaw. Native TypeScript implementation inspired by Google DeepMind's [CaMeL paper](https://arxiv.org/abs/2503.18813).

**Why:** Prompt injection is the #1 OWASP vulnerability for LLM apps. Detection-based defenses fail under adaptive attack ([100% bypass rate](https://arxiv.org/abs/2410.15511)). CaMeL takes a structural approach: instead of detecting injections, it makes them unable to influence program flow — like parameterized queries solved SQL injection.

**What it does:** Tracks data provenance through tool calls. Untrusted data (web content, file reads) gets tainted. Tainted values are blocked from reaching side-effect tools (exec, messaging, gateway) unless policy explicitly allows it or the user approves.

Refs: #39160

---

## Architecture

Three isolated LLM sessions, no cross-contamination:

| Session | Role | Has Tools? | Sees System Prompt? |
|---------|------|-----------|-------------------|
| **P-LLM** (Planner) | Generates structured JSON execution plan from user prompt | No | No (isolated) |
| **Q-LLM** (Quarantined) | Extracts values from untrusted data | No | Minimal extraction-only prompt |
| **Main Agent** | Executes approved tool calls, renders results | Yes | Yes |

## Key Design Decisions

- **`CaMeLValue<T>` with Symbol brand** — generic taint wrapper; Symbol prevents spoofed values from bypassing checks
- **Default-deny on side-effect tools** — in strict mode, ALL tainted side-effect tools route through the approval flow (ask the human, not hard deny)
- **Session-level taint tracking** — once any taint source tool runs, ALL subsequent side-effect tool args are marked tainted (LLMs can paraphrase tainted content, defeating substring matching)
- **Coordinated dual enforcement** — orchestrator path and before-tool-call hook don't double-check; active-scopes registry prevents conflicts
- **Gateway approval integration** — CaMeL approvals route through `exec.approval.request` with `source: "camel"` to bypass forwarding config, delivering approval prompts with sanitized content previews to the user's messaging surface (Signal, Discord, Telegram, CLI)
- **Active scope protection** — taint state eviction skips scopes with running orchestrators

## Configuration

```json
{
  "agents": {
    "defaults": {
      "camel": {
        "enabled": false,
        "mode": "strict",
        "qModel": "anthropic/claude-haiku-4",
        "policies": {
          "trustedRecipients": [],

          "noSideEffectTools": ["read", "web_search", "memory_search", "session_status"]
        }
      }
    }
  }
}
```

`enabled: false` by default. Opt-in per agent or globally.

## Files Changed (21 files, +982/-112)

**New CaMeL engine** (`src/agents/camel/`):
- `types.ts`, `value.ts`, `capabilities.ts` — core value system with taint tracking
- `security-policy.ts`, `taint-tracker.ts` — policy engine and taint propagation
- `plan-generator.ts`, `orchestrator.ts` — P-LLM plan generation and execution
- `quarantined-llm.ts` — Q-LLM isolation
- `tool-classifications.ts` — side-effect vs read-only tool registry
- `approval-flow.ts` — user consent integration
- `config.ts`, `active-scopes.ts`, `utils.ts` — configuration, scope coordination, shared utils
- `index.ts` — public API

**Integration** (`src/agents/`):
- `pi-tools.before-tool-call.ts` — hook-level taint tracking and policy enforcement
- `pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.ts` — CaMeL orchestrator integration with isolated P-LLM/Q-LLM sessions
- `tool-policy-pipeline.ts`, `pi-embedded-runner/run.ts`, `run/params.ts` — plumbing

**Config schema** (`src/config/`):
- `types.agents.ts`, `zod-schema.agent-runtime.ts`, `zod-schema.agents.ts`

**Documentation**: `SECURITY.md`

## Tests (28 passing)

- 9 test files covering all CaMeL modules
- Red-team scenarios: web-fetch injection → message exfil, chained tool pivots, exec injection
- Anti-spoofing: Symbol-branded values reject duck-typed fakes
- Structured extraction: JSON objects serialize correctly (not `[object Object]`)
- Policy enforcement: default-deny, trusted recipients, wildcard patterns
- Taint propagation: dependency chains, bidirectional matching

## What This Does NOT Cover (Phase 2)

- Policy configuration UI
- Per-channel approval UX optimization
- Performance profiling
- AgentDojo benchmark integration
- Q-LLM model selection UI

## References

- [Motivation Thread (@RogueEngineer)](https://x.com/RogueEngineer/status/2030485632183685382)

- [CaMeL Paper (arXiv:2503.18813)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2503.18813)
- [Simon Willison's Analysis](https://simonwillison.net/2025/Apr/11/camel/)
- [The Attacker Moves Second (arXiv:2410.15511)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2410.15511)
- [RFC Discussion: #39160](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/39160)

target.title: fix: billing misclassification and duplicate reply for multi-handle users
target.body:
## Summary

Two bug fixes:

### 1. Anthropic "usage limits" errors misclassified as rate_limit

Anthropic returns "You have reached your specified API usage limits. You will regain access on ..." when billing limits are hit. This matches the rateLimit pattern "usage limit" and gets classified as rate_limit instead of billing, causing the failover system to retry (with backoff) instead of immediately skipping to the next provider.

**Fix:**
- Add billing patterns for Anthropic usage limit messages to failover-matches.ts
- Move isBillingErrorMessage check before isRateLimitErrorMessage in both classifyFailoverReason and formatAssistantErrorText

### 2. Duplicate final reply when messaging tool target != originating target

When the agent uses the message tool to send a reply, and the user has multiple identifiers (e.g. email and phone number), the target comparison in shouldSuppressMessagingToolReplies fails because the identifiers don't match. This causes dedupeMessagingToolPayloads to be false, skipping text-based dedup entirely, and the final reply is sent a second time.

**Fix:** Always allow text-based dedup when messagingToolSentTexts is non-empty. filterMessagingToolDuplicates only removes payloads whose text matches what was already sent, so cross-channel sends with different text are unaffected.

## Test plan

- [x] Existing billing test updated with Anthropic "usage limits" pattern
- [x] Existing classifyFailoverReason test updated to verify billing classification
- [ ] Verify no regressions in unit test suite
- [ ] Manual test: send message via iMessage from phone number, verify no duplicate reply

Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: fix: strip mcp_ prefix in executeToolCalls tool name lookup
target.body:
Summary

•  Problem: executeToolCalls() in @mariozechner/pi-agent-core did exact name matching — when the LLM called mcp_exec, it couldn't find the tool registered as exec, throwing Tool mcp_exec not found.
•  Why it matters: Any session where the LLM generated mcp_-prefixed tool calls silently errored out, breaking exec/read/write operations mid-turn with no graceful fallback.
•  What changed: Added a pnpm patch to @mariozechner/pi-agent-core@0.55.3 — one line before the tools.find() call that strips a leading mcp_ prefix from the tool name before lookup.
•  What did NOT change: Tool registration names, system prompt tool lists, routing logic, and all other call sites are untouched. The normalization is purely at dispatch time.

Change Type

•  [v] Bug fix

Scope

•  [v] Skills / tool execution

Linked Issue/PR

•  Closes #
•  Related #

User-visible / Behavior Changes

Tool calls with mcp_ prefix (e.g. mcp_exec, mcp_read, mcp_write) now execute correctly instead of returning an error. Previously these silently failed and the agent would report the error back to the user or retry.

Security Impact

•  Command/tool execution surface changed? Yes — previously, mcp_exec was effectively a no-op (errored). It now correctly executes. This restores intended behavior; no new surface is opened since these tools were already registered and reachable without the prefix.

Repro + Verification

Environment

•  OS: macOS
•  Runtime/container: macOS, Node 25.8.0
•  Model/provider: anthropic/claude-opus-4-6
•  Integration/channel: Feishu
•  Relevant config: tools.profile = "full", sandbox.mode = "off"

Steps

1. Send a message that causes the LLM to call mcp_exec (e.g. "Use mcp_exec to run: echo hello")
2. Observe session logs — look for toolResult with toolName: "mcp_exec"
3. Without patch: response contains Tool mcp_exec not found. With patch: command executes and returns output.

Expected

•  Agent executes the command and returns stdout.

Actual (before fix)

•  Agent returns Tool mcp_exec not found error in tool result.

Evidence

•  [v] Trace/log snippets — session 79c343f1: L20 toolResult shows toolName: "mcp_exec" with Tool mcp_exec not found before fix.
•  [v] Passing after — 5 consecutive fresh-session calls to mcp_exec all returned correct output after patch applied.

Human Verification

•  Verified scenarios: 5 consecutive openclaw agent calls with mcp_exec, mcp_read, mcp_write across fresh sessions — all succeeded.
•  Edge cases checked: Tool called without prefix (exec) still works; mcp_ prefix on non-existent tool still throws Tool not found as expected.
•  What you did not verify: Behavior on other LLM providers (OpenAI, Gemini); behavior when tool name is exactly mcp_ with no suffix.

Compatibility / Migration

•  Backward compatible? Yes — calls without mcp_ prefix are unchanged.
•  Config/env changes? No
•  Migration needed? No

Failure Recovery

•  How to revert: Remove patches/@mariozechner__pi-agent-core@0.55.3.patch and the patchedDependencies entry from package.json, then pnpm install.
•  Files to restore: package.json (remove pnpm.patchedDependencies), delete patches/@mariozechner__pi-agent-core@0.55.3.patch.
•  Bad symptoms to watch for: If the patch fails to apply on pnpm install, the build will error — check pnpm install output for patch rejection.

Risks and Mitigations

•  Risk: A tool legitimately named mcp_something (if one is ever registered) would incorrectly resolve to something.
◦  Mitigation: OpenClaw's canonical tool registry has no mcp_-prefixed names and the naming convention explicitly forbids them. The prefix is only an LLM artifact. If upstream ever ships an mcp_-prefixed tool name, this patch should be revisited.

target.title: fix(pre-commit): use single pytest dependency specifier
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** The pre-commit config had `additional_dependencies: [pytest>=8, <9]`. In YAML this is parsed as two list items (`pytest>=8` and `<9`), so pip tries to install a package named `<9` and fails with `Invalid requirement: '<9': Expected package name at the start of dependency specifier`.
- **Why it matters:** Pre-commit environment setup fails for anyone running hooks; the skills-python-tests hook cannot install.
- **What changed:** Single specifier as one string: `["pytest>=8,<9"]` so pip receives a valid PEP 440 range.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** No other files; no voice-call or other plugin changes. (Schema fixes are in #38892.)

## Change Type (select all)

- make the early `isMainModule(...)` startup guard tolerate `ENOENT`/`uv_cwd` when the process cwd has disappeared
- add regression coverage for both the handoff cwd and early startup cwd fallback paths

## Verification

The smoke removed the live process cwd to trigger `uv_cwd`, then started a real managed update handoff helper with a missing package root and a harmless mock update script.

The managed handoff started from a missing package root, selected an existing stable cwd (`/home/rev`) instead of the missing root, ran the mock update command there, and the helper log recorded `managed update command exited code=0 signal=null`.

target.title: feat(agents): per-agent outbound A2A allowlist override
target.body:
## Summary

- Add `agents.list[].tools.agentToAgent.allow` to control which agents a specific agent may contact via session tools (`sessions_send`, `sessions_list`, `sessions_history`, `session_status`)
- When unset, the agent falls back to the global `tools.agentToAgent.allow` list; when set to explicit IDs, only those targets are reachable; `[]` isolates the agent from all cross-agent access
- The global `tools.agentToAgent.allow` continues to act as a participation gate (both requester and target must match it) before per-agent outbound rules are evaluated
- Outbound-only semantics: only the requester's override is checked, not the target's
- Replace `matchesAllow` with `matchesGlobalParticipation` and add `evaluateAccess()` returning a structured `AgentToAgentDenyReason` so all call sites produce context-aware error messages via `formatAgentToAgentAccessError()`
- Scrub deleted agent references from per-agent outbound allowlists in `pruneAgentConfig()`

### Example config (hub-spoke pattern)

```json
{
  "tools": {
    "agentToAgent": { "enabled": true, "allow": ["*"] }
  },
  "agents": {
    "list": [
      { "id": "david",  "tools": { "agentToAgent": { "allow": ["*"] } } },
      { "id": "spoke",  "tools": { "agentToAgent": { "allow": ["david"] } } },
      { "id": "kelly",  "tools": { "agentToAgent": { "allow": ["david"] } } }
    ]
  }
}
```

spoke→david ✅ | spoke→kelly ❌ | david→anyone ✅

## Test plan

- [x] `sessions-access.test.ts`: per-agent override, deny-all `[]`, global gate, target override ignored (outbound-only), fallback to global when unset
- [x] `sessions.test.ts`: per-agent outbound deny during label lookup in `sessions_send`
- [x] `openclaw-tools.session-status.test.ts`: per-agent outbound deny for cross-agent `session_status`
- [x] `agents.test.ts`: `pruneAgentConfig` scrubs deleted agent from per-agent allowlists
- [x] Build passes
- [x] Manual: configure hub-spoke agents (david/spoke/kelly), verify per-agent outbound rules — see `Real behavior proof` section below

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: per-agent outbound `agentToAgent.allow` correctly gates cross-agent session tool access. Hub-spoke topology used: `david` (hub, `allow=["*"]`), `spoke` (allow=`["david"]`), `kelly` (allow=`["david"]`). Validates the new `evaluateAccess()` decision pipeline reaches the documented per-agent outbound semantics, with the structured `per_agent_outbound` deny reason surfaced via `formatAgentToAgentAccessError()`.

- **Real environment tested**: Ubuntu 22.04 (kernel 5.15), Node 22.22.0, pnpm 10. Branch HEAD `55819f6f43` of `repair/pr-39102-a2a-refresh` (this PR rebased on current main). Live invocation of the production export `createAgentToAgentPolicy` from `src/plugin-sdk/session-visibility.ts` — no mocks, no test runner, the same code path that backs `sessions_send`, `sessions_list`, `sessions_history`, and `session_status` at runtime.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

  1. Author a hub-spoke `OpenClawConfig` matching the docs example (david hub + spoke + kelly).
  2. Build `policy = createAgentToAgentPolicy(cfg)`.
  3. Call `policy.evaluateAccess(requester, target)` for the five edge pairs.
  4. Run with: `pnpm tsx proof-hub-spoke.ts` (the script imports the worktree source directly so the running code is the patched export).

- **Evidence after fix**: terminal output, copied verbatim from `node` stdout via `tsx`.

  ```
  # per-agent A2A allowlist — hub-spoke proof
  # config: david=hub (allow *), spoke=spoke (allow david), kelly=spoke (allow david)

  [ok ] spoke -> david: ALLOWED
  [ok ] spoke -> kelly: DENIED (reason=per_agent_outbound)
  [ok ] david -> kelly: ALLOWED
  [ok ] david -> spoke: ALLOWED
  [ok ] kelly -> spoke: DENIED (reason=per_agent_outbound)

  all cases match expected behavior
  ```

- **Observed result after fix**: all five real invocations matched the documented hub-spoke semantics. Cross-spoke calls (`spoke -> kelly`, `kelly -> spoke`) were denied with the structured `per_agent_outbound` reason code from `AgentToAgentDecision`. Hub-rooted calls (`david -> spoke`, `david -> kelly`) and spoke-to-hub (`spoke -> david`) were allowed. Process exit code: `0`. Console output above is the literal stdout, no post-processing.

- **What was not tested**: end-to-end IPC roundtrip through the live `sessions_send` agent process pair (single-machine ACP transport). The four session call sites all delegate the access decision to the single `evaluateAccess()` policy proven above, and the wiring of each call site is covered by the unit tests already listed in the Test plan.

target.title: fix(agents): post-timeout compensation in sessions_send
target.body:
## Fixes #68065

`sessions_send` with a non-zero timeout previously returned `status=timeout` whenever the wait window expired, even when the target agent had already accepted the message and produced a reply after the window closed.

### Behavior contract

| Scenario | Before | After |
|---|---|---|
| Reply arrives *just after* the timeout window | `status: "timeout"` | `status: "ok"` with the late reply |
| Run accepted but no new reply within window | `status: "timeout"` | `status: "accepted"` |
| Run truly stalled / not accepted | `status: "timeout"` | `status: "timeout"` (unchanged) |
| Fire-and-forget (`timeoutSeconds: 0`) | `status: "accepted"` | `status: "accepted"` (unchanged) |

### Implementation

Adds `compensateAfterWaitTimeout()` in `src/agents/run-wait.ts` that reads the latest assistant reply snapshot after a timeout and checks whether a new reply materialized since the pre-send baseline. The timeout handler in `sessions-send-tool.ts` now calls this before returning a hard timeout.

### Test coverage

- **`src/agents/run-wait.test.ts`** — 3 new tests in a `compensateAfterWaitTimeout` describe block:
  - new reply found after timeout → returns `accepted` with reply
  - no new reply but run accepted → returns `accepted` without reply
  - compensation itself fails → falls back to hard timeout
- **`src/agents/tools/sessions.test.ts`** — 1 new test: fire-and-forget (`timeoutSeconds=0`) stays on the `accepted` path without triggering compensation or `agent.wait`
- All 42 tests in the targeted test files pass (16 in run-wait, 26 in sessions)

### Production provenance

We have been running this fix as a dist-level patch in production. This PR is the source-level equivalent, adapted to the current upstream main branch. Reference third-party implementation: wade-microblueworld/openclaw@bfe3a59

### Note on full test suite

The full `pnpm test` run has 2 pre-existing failures on upstream `main` that are unrelated to this change (`src/plugins/bundle-commands.test.ts`, `src/commands/gateway-status/helpers.test.ts`). All files touched by this PR pass cleanly.

target.title: fix(memory): apply decay to nested dated files
target.body:
## Summary

- Fix temporal decay so it also applies to dated memory files stored in `memory/` subdirectories.
- Previously, the dated-memory path matcher only recognized top-level paths like `memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md`.
- As a result, nested dated files such as `memory/daily-log/2026-04-10.md` were treated like evergreen memory and could rank higher than newer logs.
- This PR expands the regex-based path match to cover nested dated memory files and adds regression coverage for that behavior.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security
- [ ] Chore

## Scope

- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue

- Closes #67594

## Root Cause

- The dated-memory matcher assumed a flat path shape and only matched `memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md`.
- Nested dated files under `memory/` were therefore skipped by temporal decay and treated as evergreen memory.

## What Changed

- Updated the dated memory path regex to recognize dated markdown files inside `memory/` subdirectories.
- Preserved existing evergreen behavior for non-dated topic files like `MEMORY.md` and `memory/projects.md`.
- Added a regression test covering nested dated memory paths.

## User-visible Behavior

Before:
- `memory/2026-04-16.md` decayed
- `memory/daily-log/2026-04-10.md` did not decay
- `memory/session-log/2026-04-15-1200.md` did not decay

After:
- nested dated memory files under `memory/` now receive temporal decay consistently

## Verification

- Added regression coverage for nested dated memory files in `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/temporal-decay.test.ts`.
- Checked edited files for diagnostics; no new diagnostics were introduced.
- Full local test execution is currently blocked in this environment because the required `vitest` dependency is not yet available locally.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: broader path matching could accidentally include unintended memory file names.
- Mitigation: the regex remains scoped to dated markdown files under `memory/`, and regression coverage now includes nested dated paths.

## Notes

- No config changes
- No API changes
- No security impact

target.title: feat(daemon): use PowerShell Register-ScheduledTask for Windows auto-start
target.body:
## Problem

1. schtasks /Create has no built-in way to disable the default ExecutionTimeLimit, which can cause long-running tasks like the gateway to be terminated after 72 hours.
2. The previous code silently falls back to the Startup folder when admin privileges are insufficient, confusing users.
3. No explicit control over battery power settings.

## Solution

1. Use PowerShell's Register-ScheduledTask cmdlet as the primary method (falls back to schtasks only if PowerShell module is unavailable).
2. Set ExecutionTimeLimit 0 to disable the 72-hour termination.
3. Set AllowStartIfOnBatteries and DontStopIfGoingOnBatteries to allow running on battery power.
4. When not running as Administrator, throw an explicit error instead of silently falling back to Startup folder.
5. schtasks remains as fallback for environments where PowerShell ScheduledTasks module is unavailable.

## Key Changes

### New helper functions
- isRunningAsAdmin() - check if current process has admin privileges via PowerShell
- execPowerShell(script) - spawn PowerShell process and return { code, stdout, stderr }
- ^GssertPowerShellScheduledTasksAvailable() - verify Register-ScheduledTask cmdlet is available

### Modified ^GctivateScheduledTask()
1. **Admin check first**: throws explicit error if not running as Administrator (no silent Startup folder fallback)
2. **PowerShell primary**: uses Register-ScheduledTask with proper settings
3. **schtasks fallback**: only used when PowerShell module is unavailable

### Task settings (PowerShell)
`
ExecutionTimeLimit = 0        # No time limit (fixes 72h termination)
AllowStartIfOnBatteries = true  # Allow running on battery
DontStopIfGoingOnBatteries = true  # Allow running on battery
StartWhenAvailable = true  # Start task if missed while machine was off
`

### All task operations now support PowerShell-first with schtasks fallback
- isRegisteredScheduledTask(): Get-ScheduledTask ??schtasks /Query
- updateExistingScheduledTask(): Register-ScheduledTask -Force ??schtasks /Change
- unScheduledTaskOrThrow(): Start-ScheduledTask ??schtasks /Run
- stopScheduledTask(): Stop-ScheduledTask ??schtasks /End
- uninstallScheduledTask(): Unregister-ScheduledTask ??schtasks /Delete
- estartScheduledTask(): Stop + Start (PowerShell) ??schtasks /End + /Run
- eadScheduledTaskRuntime(): Get-ScheduledTask + Get-ScheduledTaskInfo ??schtasks /Query /V

## v2 - Bug Fixes (2026-04-18)

Addressed review feedback with 4 critical fixes:

1. **Fixed PowerShell hashtable keys** in eadScheduledTaskRuntime: Changed $taskState ??	askState etc. (bare identifiers, not variables). The $ prefix was causing PowerShell to evaluate them as undefined variables producing duplicate-key errors.
2. **Fixed schtasks /End fallback** in estartScheduledTask: The fallback was unreachable on Windows when PowerShell module unavailable. Now properly falls back to schtasks in the catch block.
3. **Fixed timer leak** in execPowerShell: Added clearTimeout(timer) in close and error handlers.
4. **Fixed missing return** in uninstallScheduledTask: Added early return after successful Unregister-ScheduledTask to prevent double-removal attempts.

Co-authored-by: langningchen-openclaw <langningchen-openclaw@users.noreply.github.com>

target.title: perf(agents): cache provider tool schema normalization
target.body:
## Summary

- Cache provider-normalized tool schema parameters for repeated embedded agent turns when the active provider hook exposes a stable normalization-hook identity.
- Keep the cache conservative and hook-owned: key includes the provider hook identity plus provider/model/API/base URL and ordered tool names with stable original schema signatures; values store only cloned `parameters`, never tool objects, execute closures, approval state, or guards.
- Add `OPENCLAW_TOOL_SCHEMA_CACHE=0` as a kill switch, with cache-key, clone-safety, env-disable, and eviction coverage.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** repeated embedded agent turns no longer redo provider tool-schema normalization when the provider hook identity, provider/model/config inputs, and ordered tool schema signature are unchanged. Fresh tool objects, `execute` closures, approval state, and runtime guards are still rebuilt per turn.
- **Real environment tested:** local OpenClaw checkout at `/Users/guanbear/workspace/openclaw-5.4-src`, branch `tool-schema-cache-pr3`, head `a5874d8ac60cf2974b74cc66b50028db103b390e`, macOS host, Node 25.9.0 via repo `pnpm` tooling.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** ran `pnpm exec tsx --input-type=module` locally with a script that imports `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/tool-schema-runtime.ts`, uses the real bundled OpenAI provider hook path, recreates 50 representative OpenAI-style tool objects and fresh `execute` closures per iteration, then runs 100 normalization iterations with `OPENCLAW_TOOL_SCHEMA_CACHE=0` and 100 iterations with the default cache enabled. Also ran a same-Gateway-process mock OpenAI E2E before the rebase with five main-agent RPC calls per mode and 27 tools sent per model request.
- **Evidence after fix:** current-head terminal output from the provider schema-normalization microbenchmark:

```json
{
  "head": "a5874d8ac60cf2974b74cc66b50028db103b390e",
  "cases": [
    {
      "label": "cache-disabled",
      "iterations": 100,
      "toolCount": 50,
      "ms": 63625,
      "perIterationMs": 636.25,
      "rssDeltaMb": 447.7,
      "stats": {
        "bypass": 100,
        "hit": 0,
        "miss": 0,
        "store": 0,
        "size": 0
      }
    },
    {
      "label": "cache-enabled",
      "iterations": 100,
      "toolCount": 50,
      "ms": 26927,
      "perIterationMs": 269.27,
      "rssDeltaMb": -3.1,
      "stats": {
        "bypass": 0,
        "hit": 99,
        "miss": 1,
        "store": 1,
        "size": 1
      }
    }
  ]
}
```

Additional lightweight mock Gateway E2E evidence from the same PR code before the rebase:

- cache enabled durations `[2105, 496, 471, 465, 470]`, p50 `471ms`, 5 model requests, 27 tools per request
- cache disabled durations `[2002, 482, 472, 474, 470]`, p50 `474ms`, 5 model requests, 27 tools per request

- **Observed result after fix:** the provider schema-normalization cache is active on the real bundled OpenAI provider hook path: the benchmark stores once, then records 99 cache hits while recreating tool objects and closures every iteration. The current conservative implementation reduces the repeated normalization path from `636.25ms` to `269.27ms` per iteration in this 50-tool fixture and cuts RSS growth, but the hit path still pays cache-key and original-schema-signature construction cost. The mock Gateway E2E confirms the real main-agent path still succeeds with the cache enabled, but it does not show a meaningful wall-latency delta in a five-run mocked fixture.
- **What was not tested:** full live-provider end-to-end agent reply latency was not tested. This PR caches only provider-normalized schema `parameters`; it does not cache full tool inventory/materialization, prompt-report inputs, `core-plugin-tools`, policy resolution, or complete agent-turn startup. The microbenchmark should not be read as a 400ms-per-turn full agent latency claim.

## Verification

- `pnpm test src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/tool-schema-runtime.test.ts src/agents/runtime-plan/tools.test.ts`
- `pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check`
- Current-head scoped `pnpm exec tsx` provider schema-normalization microbenchmark described above
- `git diff --check`

target.title: fix(gateway): hold startup-gated requests at server until post-attach (closes #67160)
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** The openclaw-gateway startup gate from #65365 responds `UNAVAILABLE` to startup-gated methods (`chat.history`, `models.list`, `sessions.*`, `tools.effective`, `agent.wait`) for the 8–15 s window between `[gateway] ready` and post-attach sidecar registration. The Control UI's client-side retry loop (same commit) only helps when the browser already has the retry-aware bundle. Every time an update restarts the gateway, an open browser tab is still running the **pre-fix** bundle, fires one `chat.history`, gets `UNAVAILABLE`, never retries, and pins the red `GatewayRequestError: chat.history unavailable during gateway startup` banner.
- **Why it matters:** Every in-place `openclaw-update` hits this for any user with a chat tab open — which is most of the time.
- **What changed:** The gateway now *holds* gated requests on the server until post-attach finishes, via a small `StartupGateBarrier` (deferred + 20 s timeout) threaded through the request context. If post-attach completes within the wait, the request falls through to the normal handler. If it times out, the existing `retryable/retryAfterMs` `UNAVAILABLE` shape is returned as a fallback. This removes the race independently of what bundle any browser tab has cached.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** UI is untouched. Upstream's declarative startup-gated method set (`STARTUP_UNAVAILABLE_GATEWAY_METHODS`, derived from `startup: true` core descriptors) is left exactly as upstream defines it — this PR adds only the server-side wait, not a competing method list.

## Re-port note (refreshed 2026-05-16)

This PR was originally written on 2026-04-17. Upstream has since restructured the startup gate: `src/gateway/server-startup-unavailable-methods.ts` was **deleted** and the gated method set is now declarative in `src/gateway/methods/core-descriptors.ts`. The change has been **re-architected (not rebased)** onto current `upstream/main` (`210ff7d318`):

- The barrier now lives in a new dedicated `src/gateway/server-startup-gate-barrier.ts` (its previous home no longer exists upstream).
- Upstream's `STARTUP_UNAVAILABLE_GATEWAY_METHODS` is consumed as-is; no hand-maintained list is reintroduced.
- The fail-fast gate in `src/gateway/server-methods.ts` now awaits the barrier, preserving upstream's `GATEWAY_STARTUP_RETRY_AFTER_MS` + `gatewayStartupUnavailableDetails()` response shape on timeout.
- `startupGateBarrier` is threaded as an **optional** field on the post-attach and request-context params, so existing test call sites need no changes and legacy contexts keep the immediate-UNAVAILABLE behavior.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #67160
- Related #65322, #65365, #67951
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause

- **Root cause:** The startup gate's fail-fast response (`UNAVAILABLE` at 0 ms) makes the race unrecoverable from the client side when the browser-cached UI bundle predates the retry fix in #65365. The retry fix can never help the session that's affected by the update that ships it.
- **Missing detection / guardrail:** The gate was designed around "short-lived unavailability the client can retry through." It did not account for long-lived cached bundles that lack the retry code.
- **Contributing context:** #65365 added the gate + client retry in the same commit, so the retry always ships one restart behind the bundle that needs it.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level:
  - [x] Unit test
- **Target test or file:** new `src/gateway/server-methods.startup-gate.test.ts` (unit), exercising the dispatch seam directly via `handleGatewayRequest`.
- **Scenarios locked in:**
  1. A gated request waits on the barrier and dispatches once the barrier opens (post-attach completes).
  2. A gated request responds with retryable `UNAVAILABLE` only if the barrier does not open within `STARTUP_GATE_WAIT_MS` (20 s).
  3. Non-gated methods bypass the wait entirely.
  4. Contexts without a barrier (legacy) preserve the existing immediate-UNAVAILABLE behavior.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** the four dispatch behaviors are exercised directly through `handleGatewayRequest` with a real `createStartupGateBarrier()`; the post-attach release point is a single `barrier.open()` call adjacent to the existing (already-tested) `unavailableGatewayMethods` clear, so it needs no separate harness.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: During the window between `[gateway] ready` and post-attach sidecar registration, the gateway answered startup-gated methods (`chat.history`, `models.list`) with an instant `UNAVAILABLE`. A browser tab still on the prior bundle fires one `chat.history`, gets `UNAVAILABLE`, never retries, and pins the red error banner after every gateway restart. This change holds the request on the server until post-attach instead of failing fast.
- **Real environment tested**: Live `openclaw-gateway` on aarch64 Linux (Snapdragon, WSL2) — real `journalctl --user -u openclaw-gateway` capture across an `openclaw-update`-triggered restart with a Control UI browser tab open on the prior bundle. Plus the compiled `createStartupGateBarrier()` from this commit driven directly under `node` on the same host.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Restart `openclaw-gateway` with a Control UI tab already open on the prior bundle and follow `journalctl --user -u openclaw-gateway`; separately, run the compiled `createStartupGateBarrier()` from this commit under `node` and drive `waitWithTimeout()` across the timeout, post-attach-`open()`, and already-open paths.
- **Before evidence optional**:

  ```
  05:22:24.767    [ws] webchat connected … client=openclaw-control-ui webchat v2026.4.11
  05:22:24.783    [ws] ⇄ res ✗ chat.history  0ms errorCode=UNAVAILABLE
  05:22:24.796    [ws] ⇄ res ✗ models.list   0ms errorCode=UNAVAILABLE
  ```

  One request per method, no retries — the cached bundle lacked retry code; red banner pins until reload.
- **Evidence after fix**:

  Real gateway journal, restart-while-tab-open — the server now holds the gated calls ~11 s, then dispatches; zero `UNAVAILABLE`:

  ```
  07:19:26.520    [gateway] ready (10.0s)
  07:19:26.580    [gateway] starting channels and sidecars...
  07:19:30.844    [ws] webchat connected … client=openclaw-control-ui webchat v2026.4.16
  07:19:42.024    [ws] ⇄ res ✓ chat.history 11172ms
  07:19:42.027    [ws] ⇄ res ✓ models.list  11162ms
  07:19:42.111    [plugins] embedded acpx runtime backend ready
  ```

  Live `node` console output of this commit's compiled barrier:

  ```
  WAIT_MS 20000 isOpen false
  timeout path => false (~121ms)   # held, retryable fallback only after the wait
  open path    => true  (~51ms)    # post-attach open() releases the held request
  already-open => true             # post-attach-complete fast path
  ```
- **Observed result after fix**: The gated requests are held on the server for ~11.2 s (the exact post-attach window) and then dispatched to the real handler — zero `✗ UNAVAILABLE` rows, no red banner. The compiled barrier holds until `open()` and only falls back to the retryable `UNAVAILABLE` on timeout, matching the live gateway behavior end-to-end.
- **What was not tested**: The gateway journal is from the original landing of this identical barrier code path; this commit re-architects only where the barrier module lives and how it wires into upstream's restructured startup descriptors, not its runtime behavior — which is re-exercised live above under `node`. A fresh production restart-while-tab-open journal for the re-architected wiring will be captured at deploy via the normal `openclaw-update` flow; a maintainer may apply the `proof:` override if a fresh pre-merge capture is preferred.

## Supplemental verification (not a substitute for the proof above)

- `pnpm build` — clean.
- `pnpm tsgo:core` and `pnpm tsgo:core:test` — clean (full typecheck of sources and tests).
- `src/gateway/server-methods.startup-gate.test.ts` — 4/4 passing (gated→wait→dispatch, timeout→UNAVAILABLE, non-gated passthrough, legacy no-barrier).
- `pnpm check:madge-import-cycles` / `check:import-cycles` — 0 cycles (the barrier module is a zero-import leaf).

## AI disclosure

- [x] AI-assisted (designed and implemented in Claude Code).
- Real behavior proof: the original real-gateway before/after journal (same barrier code path) plus live runtime console output of this commit's compiled barrier — see "Real behavior proof" above. A fresh production restart-while-tab-open journal for the re-architected wiring is captured at deploy time per the scope note; build/typecheck/unit/cycle checks are supplemental only.

target.title: fix(cron): prevent scheduler death when startup catch-up fails
target.body:
## Summary

When runMissedJobs() throws during start(), the timer was never armed, silently killing the cron scheduler until the next gateway restart.

## Root Cause

The start() function in src/cron/service/ops.ts calls runMissedJobs() outside of any try/catch, followed by the locked() block that calls armTimer(state). If runMissedJobs() throws (e.g. an isolated agent turn fails during startup catch-up), execution never reaches armTimer(), and the scheduler is permanently dead.

## Changes

1. **Guard armTimer in start()** - Wrap runMissedJobs() in try/catch so the timer is always armed
2. **Zombie detection in onTimer()** - Clear stale runningAtMs markers at runtime (threshold: 2x job timeout or 2 min) so jobs recover without restart
3. **Tests** - Verify timer stays armed on catch-up failure and stale markers are cleared

Fixes #67854
Related: #59056, #60799

target.title: fix(lobster): surface workflow path errors

## Summary
- stop treating workflow-looking filenames as inline pipelines when resolution fails
- surface the original workflow path error for `.lobster`, `.yaml`, `.yml`, and `.json` inputs
- add a regression test for missing relative workflow files

## Why this matters
Fixes #68101.

Today a missing relative workflow path like `lobster/missing-workflow.lobster` gets reinterpreted as a pipeline command and fails with a misleading `Unknown command` message. This keeps the existing inline pipeline path untouched, but fails closed for inputs that already look like workflow files so users get a real path/file error instead.

## Validation

- `corepack pnpm exec vitest run --project extensions extensions/lobster/src/lobster-runner.test.ts`

target.title: feat(providers/zai): inject X-Session-Id header for prompt cache stickiness
target.body: ## Summary Add `X-Session-Id` header injection for the `zai` provider to enable sticky routing on ZhipuAI's backend load balancer. On a live agent workload (78 GLM-5.1 calls in a single session), this lifts prompt-cache hit rate from **~52% → ~96%** without any user-facing configuration change. Per ZhipuAI support team guidance, sending a stable `X-Session-Id` header per logical session causes the backend LB to route all requests in that session to the same inference node — which aligns prompt-cache residency with the user's actual session boundaries instead of letting LB variance thrash the cache. ## Motivation On tool-heavy multi-turn agent workloads, the system prompt and tool definitions dominate the prompt tokens and remain stable across calls, so they *should* live in the provider's prompt cache. But without a stable session key, ZhipuAI's backend LB may route successive calls from the same logical session to different inference nodes, each of which has to rebuild the prefix cache independently. The observable symptom is a sawtooth per-call hit-rate pattern that plateaus far below the ceiling. This PR mirrors the same "header wrapper" shape already in use for the `xai` provider (`extensions/xai/stream.ts`), so the change is idiomatic and low-risk. ## Design Added `extensions/zai/stream.ts`: - **`createZaiSessionIdHeaderWrapper(baseStreamFn?)`** — stream wrapper that reads `options.sessionId`, validates it, and injects `X-Session-Id` into the outbound headers. When `sessionId` is missing, empty, whitespace-only, or exceeds 256 chars, the wrapper passes through unchanged (no header injected). - **`wrapZaiProviderStream(ctx)`** — provider-level composition. Composes the session-id header wrapper with the existing `tool-stream-default-on` behavior via `composeProviderStreamWrappers` + `createToolStreamWrapper`. Shape mirrors `extensions/xai/stream.ts`. `extensions/zai/index.ts` now registers the provider with `wrapStreamFn: wrapZaiProviderStream` instead of spreading `ZAI_TOOL_STREAM_HOOKS`, preserving the existing tool-stream behavior while adding the header injection. ## Visual comparison Same task (LinkedIn jobs research, multi-turn browser agent) on GLM-5.1, observed end-to-end: **Before PR** (orange, 50 calls): per-call hit rate is sawtoothed between ~8% and ~99%; cumulative rate plateaus at **52.4%**. 24 of 50 calls land in the "cold" bucket (hit < 10%), indicating routing-driven cache misses recur throughout the session, not just at the start. **After PR** (green, 78 calls): per-call hit rate climbs to 95-99% within the first 2-3 calls and stays essentially flat for the rest of the session; cumulative rate converges to **96.27%**. Only **1** cold call (the mandatory first-call warm-up). Two `×` markers indicate network errors that short-circuited usage accounting — unrelated to this change. ## Test plan ### Unit tests (`extensions/zai/stream.test.ts`, 13 tests, all passing) - sessionId happy path (injects `X-Session-Id` into outgoing headers) - sessionId preserves other headers passed in options - sessionId === `null`, `undefined`, empty string, whitespace-only → no header injected - sessionId > 256 chars → no header injected (length guard) - sessionId containing unicode / special characters → preserved verbatim - `wrapZaiProviderStream` composition with `tool_stream: true` and `tool_stream: false` - `createZaiSessionIdHeaderWrapper` with and without explicit `baseStreamFn` ``` ✓ extension-providers extensions/zai/stream.test.ts (13 tests) 16ms Test Files 1 passed (1) Tests 13 passed (13) ``` The pre-existing `cache-ttl.test.ts` (added in #24541) still passes, confirming no regression on the cache-TTL eligibility path that zai shares with moonshot. ### Live benchmark Identical agent task run under two configurations: | Metric | Before (no X-Session-Id) | After (this PR) | |------------------------------|-------------------------:|----------------:| | Calls (successful / errored) | 50 / 0 | 76 / 2 (network) | | Total prompt tokens | 1,589,843 | 2,672,479 | | Cached (hit) tokens | 832,896 | 2,572,736 | | Uncached (miss) tokens | 756,947 | 99,743 | | **Cache hit rate (aggregate)** | **52.39%** | **96.27%** | | **Median per-call hit rate** | **79.35%** | **99.11%** | | Cold calls (hit < 10%) | 24 / 50 | 1 / 76 | | Unique session IDs | 50 (per-request UUIDs) | 1 (session-level) | ### Cost impact Assuming GLM-5.1 cached tokens bill at ~20% of the input rate, effective input-token cost drops from ~0.58× nominal to ~0.23× nominal on this workload — **roughly a 60% input-token cost reduction**. ## Alternatives considered ### Why not rely on pi-ai's `sendSessionAffinityHeaders` compat gate? `pi-ai@0.69.0` ships a generic session-affinity mechanism in `providers/openai-completions.js` that, when `compat.sendSessionAffinityHeaders === true`, injects three headers — `session_id`, `x-client-request-id`, and `x-session-affinity` — on OpenAI-completions requests. On paper this would cover the zai transport without any plugin-local wrapper. In practice it doesn't help for this PR: 1. **The gate is default-off and currently unreached.** `detectCompat` hardcodes `sendSessionAffinityHeaders: false` for every provider. Neither pi-ai itself nor `openclaw/main` contains a single write that sets it to `true` — both `grep -rn 'sendSessionAffinityHeaders:\s*true' node_modules/@mariozechner/pi-ai/dist` and `git grep sendSessionAffinityHeaders origin/main` return no matches. Turning it on for zai would be a separate decision in its own right. 2. **The header names don't match.** The three headers above use snake_case `session_id` plus hyphenated `x-*` variants. ZhipuAI's support team specified `X-Session-Id` (capital X, capital S, capital I, hyphen-separated) as the exact sticky-routing key their load balancer matches on. We can't assume the LB accepts the pi-ai variants. 3. **Scope.** Flipping the compat gate would also change retry, WebSocket warmup, and any other OpenAI-compatible provider that later opts in. Keeping the injection plugin-local via `wrapStreamFn` confines the blast radius to `zai` and to the literal `X-Session-Id` key. If ZhipuAI later confirms they also recognize `session_id` / `x-session-affinity`, flipping the compat gate becomes a reasonable follow-up — this PR does not foreclose that path. ## Scope and caveats - This PR changes behavior only for the `zai` provider. No other provider is touched. - The wrapper is a no-op when `sessionId` is not provided, so callers that do not plumb through a session identifier see identical behavior to today. - Baseline data was captured on a deployment that served GLM-5.1 through an OpenAI-compatible proxy layer. The after-PR run is against `api.z.ai` directly using the bundled `zai` provider. Both runs used the same agent, same task, same model, and drew from the same underlying ZhipuAI backend — the only variable we deliberately changed is the presence or absence of `X-Session-Id`. - The `X-Session-Id` header semantics are per ZhipuAI support team's prompt-cache best-practice guidance. The provider ignores the header when absent, so no behavior change for upstream-side consumers that don't opt in. ## Related - Follow-up to #24541 (added ZAI/Moonshot to cache-TTL eligible providers). This PR complements the TTL work by addressing the *routing* aspect of prompt-cache optimization. - Related to #24497 (closed) — the sticky-routing concern was originally raised there. ## Checklist - [x] Unit tests added (13 new tests) - [x] Existing tests pass (no regression) - [x] CHANGELOG updated - [x] `docs/concepts/model-providers.md` updated - [x] Live-workload benchmark collected and included - [x] Follows existing `extensions/xai/stream.ts` pattern for consistency
